proposals.title: Sparse, Adaptive Sampling for Efficient Object Recognition: Replacing the Sliding Window with a Learned World Model
proposals.motivation: The multiscale sliding window approach, as used in OverFeat and many subsequent detectors, is fundamentally inefficient, performing redundant computations over uniform, non-informative image regions. This computational burden limits real-time applications and energy efficiency, especially on mobile platforms. A paradigm shift towards sparse, data-dependent computation is needed to scale recognition systems to more complex, real-world environments.
proposals.hypothesis: A neural network, guided by a learned internal world model of scene composition, can directly and sparsely predict a small set of informative image regions for detailed processing, thereby eliminating the need for exhaustive sliding-window search without sacrificing detection accuracy.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage, end-to-end trainable architecture. The first stage is a global context encoder that processes a downsampled full image and outputs parameters for a learned spatial sampler (e.g., a Spatial Transformer network or a set of bounding box proposals). This sampler is trained using a combination of reconstruction loss (from a generative world model component) and a downstream task loss (e.g., multi-label classification). The second stage is a high-resolution expert network that processes only these sparsely sampled, potentially transformed image patches for final semantic prediction, using techniques from multi-task learning to share representations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete framework for replacing brute-force sliding windows with adaptive, model-based sampling, significantly reducing FLOPs in the inference pipeline. It will demonstrate how self-supervised world model objectives can guide efficient attention in discriminative tasks. The resulting method will be a step towards more autonomous, efficient visual systems that allocate computation dynamically.
proposals.novelty: While attention mechanisms and region proposal networks exist, this approach uniquely integrates a generative world model objective—inspired by LeCun's recent work—explicitly to learn the sampling policy, making the sparsity adaptive to scene semantics rather than being a fixed heuristic. It directly challenges the architectural necessity of the sliding window, not just its efficiency.

proposals.title: Anisotropic Feature Pooling: Learning Adaptive Region Representations via Image Pyramids
proposals.motivation: Standard deep learning pipelines for object detection and recognition warp variable-aspect-ratio regions of interest (RoIs) to fixed squares, introducing unnatural shape distortions that degrade representation learning. This is a fundamental limitation in pre-2026 vision systems, as it misaligns with the true geometry of objects in the world, hindering precise localization and semantic understanding. Drawing an analogy to computer graphics, where MIP mapping and anisotropic filtering adapt sampling to surface orientation to prevent aliasing and distortion, we propose a similar adaptive mechanism for neural feature extraction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that replacing fixed-shape RoI pooling with a learnable, anisotropic pooling filter operating on a feature pyramid will yield more geometrically faithful and semantically richer representations, leading to improved performance on tasks requiring precise localization and recognition of non-square objects.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel pooling module that takes a region proposal (with bounding box and optional coarse segmentation) and a multi-scale feature pyramid (e.g., from a backbone like a ResNet). Instead of warping, a small neural network (a filter predictor) will analyze the region's geometry and content to predict parameters for a 2D anisotropic Gaussian sampling kernel. This kernel will be applied to sample and aggregate features adaptively from the appropriate pyramid levels, akin to anisotropic filtering, producing a fixed-size output tensor. The entire system, including the filter predictor, will be trained end-to-end using a combination of supervised detection loss and a self-supervised objective that encourages the pooled features to be predictive of the region's true aspect ratio and shape.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, geometrically-aware building block for vision architectures that moves beyond the fixed-aspect-ratio paradigm. It will provide a principled method for learning invariant representations from variable-shaped regions, directly improving tasks like object detection and instance segmentation. Furthermore, it will bridge concepts from classical computer graphics sampling theory with modern deep representation learning.
proposals.novelty: While feature pyramids and deformable convolutions exist, the explicit formulation of pooling as a learnable, anisotropic sampling operation guided by region geometry is novel. The key innovation is the direct analogy to graphics texture filtering, leading to a dedicated filter predictor network that makes sampling adaptive and differentiable.

proposals.title: Learning Aspect-Ratio Invariant World Models for Object Detection via Graph Wavelet Neural Networks
proposals.motivation: Current object detectors rely on fixed input grids or aspect-ratio warping, which distorts object geometry and degrades localization, especially for irregular or multi-scale objects. This is a fundamental limitation for learning robust, generalizable visual representations, a core theme in self-supervised learning. A model that can process visual structure in its native geometry would better align with the goal of learning world models that reflect physical object properties.
proposals.hypothesis: By representing region proposals as irregular graphs and performing multiscale feature extraction via spectral graph wavelets, we can learn object representations that are invariant to aspect ratio and scale, leading to superior localization accuracy and more geometrically faithful feature embeddings.
proposals.method: We will develop a Graph Wavelet Neural Network (GWNN) detector. Region proposals from a first-stage network will be converted into graphs where nodes are local feature patches connected by edges based on spatial proximity. We will implement fast, localized spectral graph convolutions using polynomial approximations of wavelet kernels (e.g., Hammond et al., 2011; Defferrard et al., 2016) to extract multi-resolution features directly on each variable-shaped graph. This architecture will be trained end-to-end, combining a self-supervised objective for graph representation learning (inspired by the 'cookbook' of SSL) with a supervised detection loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new object detection paradigm that eliminates geometric distortion, advancing the field of learning from images with variable structure. It will provide a concrete method for applying spectral graph theory and multiresolution analysis to core computer vision tasks, yielding more semantically meaningful and geometrically consistent visual representations.
proposals.novelty: The novel fusion lies in applying localized spectral graph wavelets—typically used in non-visual domains—to the specific problem of aspect-ratio invariant object detection, moving beyond standard graph convolutional networks (GCNs) or spatial transformers to a principled multiscale analysis on irregular visual regions.

proposals.title: Learning Aspect-Ratio-Invariant Visual Representations via Dynamic Spatial Pooling and JEPA-style Prediction
proposals.motivation: Standard convolutional networks and vision transformers require fixed-size, often square, input images, forcing geometric distortion of objects and scenes. This distortion corrupts the intrinsic structure of visual data, harming tasks requiring precise spatial understanding, such as object localization, segmentation, and 3D inference. Relaxing this constraint is a fundamental step towards learning more accurate world models from visual data, a core theme in self-supervised representation learning.
proposals.hypothesis: A neural architecture that natively processes variable-aspect-ratio image regions without distortion, combined with a self-supervised objective that enforces spatial consistency, will learn visual representations that significantly improve geometric and semantic understanding for downstream tasks.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stream architecture building on pre-2026 concepts. The core is a variable-aspect-ratio encoder using spatial pyramid pooling or adaptive pooling layers to produce fixed-dimensional feature vectors from arbitrary rectangular regions. This will be integrated into a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA), where the objective is to predict the representation of a target image patch from a context patch of a different, randomly sampled aspect ratio. The prediction will be performed in the latent space, enforcing invariance to non-essential geometric variations while preserving semantic and structural information.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel network design and training recipe for aspect-ratio-agnostic visual representation learning. It will deliver improved benchmarks on tasks sensitive to object geometry, such as Pascal VOC object detection and COCO instance segmentation, and advance the development of self-supervised methods that respect the natural statistics of visual data.
proposals.novelty: While adaptive pooling exists, its integration into a modern self-supervised framework (JEPA) specifically to eliminate aspect-ratio distortion as a core inductive bias is novel. This moves beyond simple data augmentation to a fundamental architectural change guided by a predictive, world-modeling objective.

proposals.title: Joint World Modeling via Geometric-Semantic Feature Co-Regularization
proposals.motivation: Current self-supervised and supervised representation learning often prioritizes semantic classification, leading to features that are suboptimal for geometric tasks like segmentation and detection. Conversely, training solely for geometric precision yields features lacking semantic richness. This decoupling contradicts the integrated nature of perception in autonomous systems, which require a unified understanding of both 'what' and 'where'.
proposals.hypothesis: By co-regularizing a shared feature space with simultaneous geometric and semantic objectives, we can learn a unified representation that is superior for both localization and classification tasks compared to models trained on either objective sequentially or in isolation.
proposals.method: We will train a single backbone network (e.g., a Vision Transformer) with a dual-branch decoder. The network will be optimized under a combined loss: a geometric precision term (e.g., pixel-wise contrastive loss or segmentation IoU) and a semantic discrimination term (cross-entropy for image-level classification). A core component is a feature consistency regularizer, such as a mutual information maximization term or a contrastive alignment loss, applied between the intermediate feature maps of the two branches to enforce geometric-semantic coherence. Training will leverage multi-task datasets like COCO and ImageNet.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for learning unified visual representations that bridge the gap between high-level semantics and low-level geometry. It will deliver a model that sets a new state-of-the-art on combined benchmarks for segmentation, detection, and classification, advancing towards more holistic world models for autonomous perception.
proposals.novelty: The approach inverts the standard classification-first paradigm by treating geometric and semantic learning as co-equal and concurrent objectives from the start, enforced via explicit feature-space co-regularization, rather than simple multi-task learning or sequential fine-tuning.

proposals.title: Geometric Scaffolding: Learning Robust Visual Representations via Structure-First Pretraining
proposals.motivation: Current self-supervised and supervised visual representation learning is dominated by tasks like image classification or instance discrimination, which prioritize semantic categorization over precise spatial understanding. This creates a potential misalignment for downstream tasks requiring strong geometric reasoning, such as object detection, depth estimation, or autonomous navigation. Inspired by the 'structure-first' paradigm in protein design, we hypothesize that initializing networks with a deep understanding of scene geometry will yield more robust and generalizable visual features.
proposals.hypothesis: A convolutional neural network pretrained on a dense geometric prediction task (e.g., panoramic depth estimation) will learn visual representations that transfer more robustly and generalize better to a wider range of downstream tasks, especially those requiring spatial awareness, compared to a network pretrained on semantic classification.
proposals.method: We will pretrain a standard convolutional architecture (e.g., a ResNet-50) using a self-supervised objective for panoramic depth estimation from single images on a large-scale dataset like ImageNet, without labels. This task forces the model to infer 3D structure from 2D cues. We will also explore a multi-task geometric pretraining regime combining depth, surface normals, and optical flow estimation from video. The resulting 'structure-first' backbone will be frozen and its representations evaluated by training linear probes or fine-tuning on standard benchmarks including ImageNet classification, COCO object detection, and NYUv2 depth estimation.
proposals.contribution: This work will challenge the established notion that classification-derived features are the optimal universal starting point. It aims to establish a new pretraining paradigm based on geometric understanding, potentially leading to more data-efficient and robust models for vision-based robotics and AR/VR. The results will provide empirical evidence for the value of 'structure-first' learning in artificial neural networks.
proposals.novelty: While geometric tasks are used in multi-task learning or as auxiliary losses, proposing them as the sole, primary pretraining objective to build a general-purpose visual backbone inverts the standard paradigm. This directly tests the analogy from structural biology in a deep learning context, moving beyond incremental multi-task additions.

proposals.title: Geometric-Primed Representations: A Self-Supervised Framework for Task-Agnostic Feature Learning via Dense Prediction
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm in visual representation learning, exemplified by OverFeat and ImageNet pre-training, uses classification as a pretext task, assuming these features transfer well to geometric tasks like detection and segmentation. However, classification-focused features may discard low-level spatial and structural information crucial for precise localization. Inverting this paradigm could yield more versatile, geometry-aware foundational representations, aligning with the push for more general, self-supervised world models.
proposals.hypothesis: A foundational visual representation network, first optimized through self-supervised dense prediction tasks (e.g., image inpainting, jigsaw puzzle solving, or contrastive pixel-level tasks), will yield features that are more transferable and effective for a broader range of downstream tasks—especially geometric ones—compared to representations learned from a classification-first objective.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-task self-supervised framework where a convolutional encoder (e.g., a ResNet variant) is trained primarily on pixel-level reconstruction and prediction tasks that enforce spatial understanding, such as a non-contrastive masked image modeling objective. This 'geometric priming' phase will be followed by a lightweight adaptation phase, where simple projection heads are trained for classification, detection, and segmentation on standard benchmarks (ImageNet, COCO, PASCAL VOC). We will systematically compare the sample efficiency and final performance of this inverted paradigm against standard classification-pre-trained and modern self-supervised (e.g., SimCLR, BYOL) baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous empirical evaluation of the 'localization-first' pre-training hypothesis, potentially establishing a new strong baseline for representation learning. It will contribute a publicly available model and training recipe to the self-supervised 'cookbook' that prioritizes geometric consistency, which could be particularly valuable for robotics and autonomous navigation where precise spatial reasoning is paramount.
proposals.novelty: While multi-task and self-supervised learning are well-studied, systematically inverting the classic 'classification then localization' pipeline to prioritize dense prediction as the core representation-forming objective is a non-obvious and under-explored direction that challenges a deeply entrenched design heuristic in computer vision.

proposals.title: Conflict-Aware Gradient Harmonization for Unified Visual World Models
proposals.motivation: End-to-end learning for unified vision tasks (classification, localization, detection) is hindered by optimization conflicts between tasks, leading to sub-optimal shared representations and performance plateaus. Pre-2026 multi-task learning often relies on heuristic or static loss balancing, which fails to adapt to dynamic gradient interactions during training. Resolving this conflict is essential for building robust, general-purpose visual systems that learn coherent world models from diverse supervisory signals.
proposals.hypothesis: A training framework that dynamically analyzes and harmonizes conflicting task gradients in real-time will enable more effective feature sharing and consistently outperform both separate networks and naively joint-trained models on a unified vision task suite.
proposals.method: We propose a gradient coordination module inserted into the backward pass of a shared backbone network (e.g., a ResNet or Vision Transformer). At each step, it computes task-specific loss gradients and applies a conflict-aware algorithm: first, it measures pairwise gradient alignment via cosine similarity; second, it modulates gradients using techniques like gradient projection (to remove conflicting components) or adaptive weighting based on gradient magnitude and direction. This ensures updates follow a consensus direction, promoting cooperative optimization. The unified architecture will output classification, bounding box, and segmentation masks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, optimization-centric solution to multi-task conflict in vision, moving beyond ad-hoc loss weighting. It will deliver a robust training framework for unified models and offer new insights into gradient dynamics in joint learning. The resulting models should demonstrate superior performance and representation quality on benchmarks like COCO and PASCAL VOC.
proposals.novelty: While gradient surgery exists in NLP, its application to dense, unified vision tasks with real-time, conflict-aware modulation is novel. This approach directly operationalizes the analysis of gradient interference—a known but often heuristically addressed problem—into a core training mechanism for visual world models.

proposals.title: Unified Lagrangian Multi-Task Learning for Vision: Adaptive Loss Balancing via a Meta-Objective
proposals.motivation: Multi-task learning (MTL) in computer vision, such as joint classification, detection, and segmentation, is crucial for efficient, generalizable representations. However, pre-2026 methods rely on static, manually-tuned loss weightings or complex gradient balancing heuristics, which are suboptimal and hinder the discovery of a truly shared, robust feature space. This limits the potential for models to autonomously learn the intrinsic structure and trade-offs between tasks, a core challenge in self-supervised and representation learning.
proposals.hypothesis: A unified, trainable Lagrangian objective, where a lightweight meta-network dynamically infers optimal task-weighting coefficients, will enable more effective feature sharing and superior joint optimization compared to static MTL baselines, leading to improved performance and generalization across vision tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a primary vision backbone (e.g., a ResNet or Vision Transformer) with multiple task-specific heads. The key innovation is a Lagrangian meta-network, a small MLP, that takes as input a representation of the current training state (e.g., task loss gradients or feature statistics) and outputs adaptive Lagrange multipliers (λ_i) for each task loss. The total loss is L_total = Σ λ_i(t) * L_i, where λ_i(t) are learned parameters of the meta-network. The entire system, including the meta-network, is trained end-to-end by optimizing a combined objective that includes both the primary task losses and a regularization term on the λ_i to prevent collapse, inspired by constrained optimization in control theory.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) A novel, principled framework for adaptive multi-task loss balancing grounded in Lagrangian mechanics, moving beyond heuristic tuning. 2) Empirical evidence on standard vision benchmarks (e.g., NYUv2, PASCAL-Context) that this method yields superior task performance and more transferable representations than fixed-weight MTL or gradient surgery methods. 3) A step towards systems that can automatically discover task relationships during learning, aligning with the pursuit of more autonomous, world-model-driven AI.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in framing multi-task learning as the optimization of a unified Lagrangian system with trainable constraints, where the balancing mechanism itself is a learned function of the model's state, rather than a fixed or manually designed policy.

proposals.title: Joint Energy-Based Training for Unified Visual Recognition: Integrating Classification, Localization, and Detection
proposals.motivation: Current computer vision pipelines often train separate models for classification, localization, and detection, leading to inefficiency and suboptimal feature sharing. A unified, end-to-end training framework would better reflect the integrated nature of visual understanding, potentially improving data efficiency and model robustness. This aligns with the pre-2026 push towards more general, multi-task learning systems and self-supervised world models.
proposals.hypothesis: A single neural network, trained with a unified objective function that combines classification, localization, and detection losses within an energy-based or joint embedding framework, will learn more transferable and semantically richer visual representations, leading to superior performance on all three tasks compared to separately trained or sequentially fine-tuned models.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-head architecture built on a shared, deep convolutional or transformer-based backbone. The unified training objective will be a weighted sum of a classification cross-entropy loss, a pixel-wise regression loss for dense localization, and a set-based detection loss (e.g., inspired by DETR). Crucially, we will explore an energy-based model (EBM) formulation to regularize the joint latent space, where the energy function is defined by the compatibility between the image representation and the structured output (class, box). Training will leverage techniques from self-supervised learning 'cookbooks' to stabilize the joint optimization.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, end-to-end trainable model for core visual recognition tasks, demonstrating the benefits of joint optimization. It will provide empirical evidence on how feature representations evolve under a unified objective versus task-specific training. The resulting framework and analysis will be a step towards more general, efficient, and capable vision systems.
proposals.novelty: While multi-task learning exists, a truly joint, end-to-end formulation from raw pixels for these three specific, structured tasks—especially using an energy-based model to unify their disparate outputs—is unexplored. It moves beyond simple parameter sharing to a principled, single-objective optimization of the combined task space.

proposals.title: Temporal OverFeat: Integrated Recognition, Localization, and Detection in Video via Spatiotemporal Feature Learning
proposals.motivation: The OverFeat framework demonstrated the power of integrated, multi-scale convolutional networks for recognition, localization, and detection in static images. However, video data, which constitutes a vast portion of real-world perceptual input, contains rich temporal structure and coherence that is largely untapped by such static models. Leveraging this temporal information is crucial for building more accurate, efficient, and robust vision systems for applications like autonomous navigation and video understanding, which are central to Yann LeCun's research on learning world models.
proposals.hypothesis: Extending the OverFeat architecture to explicitly model temporal dependencies across video frames will yield significant improvements in accuracy and efficiency for integrated recognition, localization, and detection tasks, by leveraging the inherent predictability and consistency of the visual world over time.
proposals.method: We propose to adapt the OverFeat convolutional network into a spatiotemporal architecture. This involves replacing 2D convolutions and pooling with 3D counterparts in early layers to capture local motion, and designing novel recurrent connections or temporal convolutional layers atop the feature hierarchy to integrate information across longer sequences. We will explore training strategies from self-supervised learning, such as temporal contrastive predictive coding, to learn robust spatiotemporal representations from unlabeled video. The integrated detection and localization pipeline will be modified to propagate hypotheses across frames, using temporal coherence as a regularizer.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a unified, efficient framework for video understanding that performs recognition, localization, and detection in a single forward pass, extending the original OverFeat contribution to the temporal domain. It will advance methods for learning from temporal data without dense supervision, contributing to the development of more autonomous learning systems. The resulting models and insights will directly inform research in autonomous navigation and world model learning.
proposals.novelty: While 3D CNNs and two-stream networks exist, the novelty lies in the integrated, multi-task design philosophy of OverFeat—applied to video—and the explicit focus on leveraging temporal coherence not just for classification, but for efficient, accurate localization and detection within a single learned model.

proposals.title: Consensus-Driven Object Proposal Fusion: A Robust Alternative to Greedy NMS
proposals.motivation: Current object detection pipelines rely heavily on greedy, heuristic post-processing steps like Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) to merge duplicate bounding boxes. These methods are sensitive to hyperparameters like IoU thresholds and the processing order of detections, leading to instability and sub-optimal performance, especially in cluttered scenes. A more robust, data-driven fusion mechanism is needed to improve the reliability of final object hypotheses, particularly for self-supervised and multi-task learning systems that generate diverse, multi-scale predictions.
proposals.hypothesis: By reframing bounding box fusion as a consensus clustering problem, where multiple randomized merging trials generate an association matrix, we can derive a more stable and accurate set of final object proposals than standard NMS.
proposals.method: We will adapt consensus clustering techniques from bioinformatics. For a set of raw detections, we will run hundreds of randomized merging iterations, each varying parameters (IoU threshold, spatial weighting, box ordering). Each iteration outputs a set of merged boxes, forming a partition. A co-association matrix will be built, where each cell records the frequency that two raw detections co-occur in the same final box. We will then apply spectral clustering or hierarchical agglomerative clustering on this matrix to extract the consensus, high-confidence object proposals. This process will be integrated as a plug-in module for standard detection frameworks like Detectron2.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, robust alternative to NMS that reduces order-dependence and hyperparameter sensitivity. It will offer a new perspective on post-processing as a consensus problem, potentially improving evaluation metrics like mAP in crowded benchmarks. The method aligns with the broader theme of learning stable world representations by making the final object output stage more reliable.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the direct analogy from bioinformatics consensus clustering to a core computer vision task, introducing a randomized, ensemble-based approach to a problem traditionally solved by deterministic heuristics. This cross-disciplinary transfer is non-obvious within the object detection literature pre-2026.

proposals.title: Self-Supervised Learning of Object Proposals via Differentiable Multi-Scale Box Merging
proposals.motivation: In object detection and self-supervised representation learning, generating high-quality object proposals is a foundational step. Current multi-scale detection pipelines often rely on heuristic, non-learned merging strategies (e.g., greedy NMS) to consolidate bounding box predictions, which can discard semantically meaningful detections and introduce localization errors. This limits the quality of the supervisory signals derived from unsupervised tasks like pretext-based detection or world model learning, hindering the acquisition of robust visual representations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a merging strategy for object proposals can be learned directly from data in a self-supervised manner, and that integrating this differentiable merging module into a multi-scale detection pipeline will yield more semantically coherent and precise object proposals, thereby improving downstream representation quality.
proposals.method: We will design a neural merging module that operates on the set of candidate bounding boxes from a feature pyramid. This module will use a transformer-based architecture to model pairwise box relations, leveraging geometric and appearance features. It will be trained with a self-supervised objective, such as maximizing the mutual information between merged proposal features and the global scene context from a momentum encoder, inspired by contrastive learning principles. The entire system, including the backbone and merging network, will be trained end-to-end without manual labels, using a reconstruction or consistency loss across augmented views.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, learnable alternative to fixed post-processing algorithms like NMS, directly integrated into self-supervised learning pipelines. It will provide a method to generate higher-fidelity object proposals from unsupervised data, which can serve as improved training signals for world model prediction or semantic representation learning tasks. The resulting system aims to advance the state-of-the-art in autonomous perception by making the proposal generation process itself a learnable component of the representation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in formulating bounding box merging not as a post-processing heuristic but as a differentiable, self-supervised learning problem. While prior work has focused on improving detectors or representation learning objectives, directly learning to merge multi-scale detections in an unsupervised framework is an unexplored direction that bridges geometric reasoning with semantic feature learning.

proposals.title: JEPA-Inspired Adaptive Proposal Merging via End-to-End Learnable Graph Attention
proposals.motivation: Current object detection pipelines rely on heuristic post-processing steps like Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) to merge duplicate bounding box proposals. These hand-crafted rules are brittle, cannot adapt to specific object structures or occlusion patterns, and create a disconnect between the detector's training objective and the final output. A learnable merging mechanism, aligned with the principles of Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) for learning robust world models, is needed to create more coherent and adaptive perceptual systems.
proposals.hypothesis: That framing proposal merging as an end-to-end learnable graph reasoning task, where a neural module predicts interactions between candidate boxes, will yield more accurate and context-aware final detections than fixed algorithms like NMS, especially under complex scenes with occlusion and varying scales.
proposals.method: We construct a fully-connected graph where nodes are candidate bounding boxes from an upstream detector (e.g., a DETR variant). Each node is featurized with its visual appearance (from a backbone CNN) and geometric properties. A Graph Attention Network (GATv2) or a lightweight Transformer processes this graph, using learned attention to compute weighted messages between nodes. This module outputs a refined set of boxes and confidence scores. The entire system—detector backbone, proposal generator, and GAT merging module—is trained end-to-end using a multi-task loss combining detection and a regularization term encouraging coherent merging, inspired by self-supervised representation learning objectives.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a fully differentiable, adaptive alternative to NMS, integrating post-processing into the core learning loop. It will demonstrate how structured reasoning over object proposals, guided by learned attention, can improve detection metrics on challenging benchmarks (COCO, LVIS). Furthermore, it will offer a concrete instance of applying JEPA-like predictive world modeling principles to a low-level perceptual grouping task.
proposals.novelty: While graph networks have been used for post-processing, integrating them as an end-to-end, jointly trainable component with the detector to perform adaptive *merging* (rather than just refinement) is novel. This moves beyond fixed geometric rules to a data-driven, semantic-aware consolidation process directly informed by the visual representation learning objectives championed by LeCun.

proposals.title: Multi-Object Detection as a Spin System: A Potts Model for Globally Optimal Box Consensus
proposals.motivation: Object detection systems rely heavily on heuristic post-processing steps like Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) to merge redundant bounding box proposals. NMS is brittle, sensitive to thresholds, and lacks a principled global view, leading to missed detections in crowded scenes or suppression of accurate but overlapping predictions. A robust, learnable, and globally coherent merging mechanism is a critical unsolved problem for reliable perception in autonomous systems and multi-object scene understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: Framing box consensus as a multi-label assignment problem solvable via a Potts model energy minimization will yield more accurate and robust final detections than greedy, local NMS heuristics, particularly in dense and occluded scenarios.
proposals.method: We will formulate each candidate bounding box from a detector as a spin in a Potts model, with its state representing assignment to a unique final detection cluster. An energy function will be defined with unary terms from objectness/classification scores and pairwise terms modeling interactions based on IoU, semantic feature similarity (from a backbone network), and spatial consistency. Inference for the optimal assignment will be performed using efficient multi-label graph cut algorithms or loopy belief propagation. This module will be integrated as a differentiable layer within an end-to-end trainable detection framework (e.g., Faster R-CNN or DETR variants) to learn potential functions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, physics-inspired framework for box merging that replaces heuristics with a principled energy-based model. It will advance the field by introducing a trainable, globally consistent consensus mechanism, potentially improving benchmark performance on challenging datasets like COCO and LVIS. The resulting 'consensus layer' could become a reusable component for various detection and grouping tasks.
proposals.novelty: While energy-based models are known in vision, applying the Potts model formalism specifically to the low-level box merging problem is novel. This moves beyond viewing NMS as a filtering step to recasting it as a collective inference problem on a graph of predictions, leveraging tools from statistical physics.

proposals.title: Structured Prediction for Object Detection: A CRF Framework for Optimal Box Merging
proposals.motivation: Current object detection pipelines, especially in dense or multi-scale scenarios, produce numerous overlapping candidate boxes. The final merging step, typically handled by heuristic non-maximum suppression (NMS), is a critical bottleneck. NMS makes simplistic, local decisions that can suppress valid detections in crowded scenes or produce sub-optimal bounding box fits, limiting the precision of state-of-the-art detectors. A principled, learnable approach to this merging problem is needed to advance detection robustness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling the dependencies between overlapping candidate bounding boxes as a Conditional Random Field (CRF) will allow for globally optimal inference, leading to more accurate final detections than greedy, local NMS heuristics. This structured prediction framework can be integrated into modern self-supervised and multi-task visual representation learning systems.
proposals.method: We will formulate the box merging task as inference on a graph where nodes are candidate boxes and edges connect overlapping proposals. Node potentials will encode individual box confidence scores from a backbone detector (e.g., a pre-trained self-supervised model). Pairwise potentials will model compatibility between boxes, learning to penalize redundant detections and encourage precise localization. We will implement a fully differentiable, efficient mean-field inference algorithm for the CRF, enabling end-to-end training alongside a representation learning backbone. The model will be evaluated on standard detection benchmarks (COCO, LVIS) against NMS and its learned variants.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, trainable replacement for NMS that leverages structured probabilistic reasoning, directly improving detection metrics like AP in crowded scenes. It will demonstrate how graphical model inference can be seamlessly integrated into deep learning-based perception systems. The resulting framework will be a step towards more coherent and globally reasoned visual scene understanding.
proposals.novelty: While CRFs are well-established in semantic segmentation for pixel-label consistency, their application to the distinct problem of bounding box selection and merging in object detection is novel. This approach moves beyond post-processing heuristics to a principled, learnable component of the detection pipeline.

proposals.title: Joint Energy-Based World Models for Fallback-Guaranteed Adaptive Visual Search
proposals.motivation: Autonomous systems relying on learned attention for visual search risk catastrophic failure if the model ignores critical regions, a known vulnerability in adversarial or out-of-distribution scenarios. While exhaustive search is robust, it is computationally prohibitive for real-time applications like autonomous navigation. A principled method is needed to combine the efficiency of adaptive computation with the guaranteed coverage of classical computer vision.
proposals.hypothesis: A dual-stream architecture, governed by a joint energy-based world model, can dynamically and reliably switch from an efficient attention-driven search to a guaranteed-coverage exhaustive recovery, maintaining robustness without sacrificing average-case efficiency.
proposals.method: We propose a system with a fast, saliency-based proposal network (Stream A) and a concurrent, low-resolution sliding-window feature extractor (Stream B). Their outputs are integrated into a latent world model, trained via contrastive or masked prediction tasks to estimate a joint energy function. A decision module, formulated as uncertainty estimation within this energy-based framework, monitors the model's certainty. If energy or uncertainty exceeds a threshold, it triggers Stream B to execute a dynamic, multi-scale exhaustive search, reallocating computational resources to guarantee task completion.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal framework for building vision systems with verifiable fallback guarantees, advancing robust autonomous perception. It contributes a novel training paradigm for world models that explicitly reason about search failure modes and resource allocation, bridging self-supervised representation learning and reliable systems engineering.
proposals.novelty: The integration of an energy-based world model as the arbitrator for triggering exhaustive recovery is novel, moving beyond simple confidence thresholds. This creates a unified latent space where the efficiency of learned attention and the coverage of exhaustive search are jointly optimized, a non-obvious synthesis given the typical separation between these paradigms.

proposals.title: Efficient Visual Search via Reinforcement Learning of Optimal Foraging Policies
proposals.motivation: Exhaustive sliding-window search for object detection is computationally wasteful, especially for real-time applications like autonomous navigation. While attention mechanisms and saliency models offer improvements, they often lack a principled, sequential decision-making framework for deciding where and how long to look. This project addresses the core efficiency problem in visual search by drawing a formal analogy to biological foraging strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: An agent trained with reinforcement learning to emulate optimal foraging heuristics—specifically, the marginal value theorem—can learn an adaptive visual search policy that dynamically allocates computational resources, significantly outperforming fixed-search baselines in speed-accuracy trade-offs.
proposals.method: We will train a recurrent agent (e.g., a GRU-based controller) using an actor-critic RL algorithm (e.g., A3C or PPO). The agent's state will be a learned representation fusing a multi-scale feature pyramid (from a backbone like ResNet-50) encoding scene context, a history of its past fixations, and a low-level saliency prior. At each step, the agent selects a bounding box region to process with a high-capacity classifier/detector. The reward function combines a positive signal for correct detections and a negative cost for each computational step, incentivizing efficient search. The 'giving-up time'—when to leave a patch—will emerge from the learned value function.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, ecologically-inspired framework for adaptive visual search that explicitly optimizes the speed-accuracy trade-off. It will yield a new benchmark for efficient object detection and search, demonstrating how principles from behavioral ecology can inform the design of more efficient and autonomous computer vision systems.
proposals.novelty: While RL has been used for visual attention, the formal grounding in Optimal Foraging Theory and the explicit modeling of patch residence time (via the marginal value theorem) provides a novel, biologically-plausible objective for learning when to terminate search in a region, moving beyond simple saliency or hard attention.

proposals.title: Learning to Query: Attention-Guided Inverse Graph Inference for Scalable Structure Discovery
proposals.motivation: Inferring the latent relational structure of complex systems from observed data is a fundamental inverse problem in fields like network science, neuroscience, and multi-agent systems. Exhaustive search over possible graph topologies is computationally intractable for large systems. Current pre-2026 methods for graph structure learning often rely on statistical priors or continuous relaxations that lack a strategic, sample-efficient mechanism for exploring the vast space of potential edges, limiting scalability and interpretability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a learned attention mechanism, inspired by models of visual saliency and sequential decision-making, can strategically guide the search for latent graph edges by focusing computational queries on the most informative potential connections, thereby solving the inverse graph inference problem more efficiently and accurately than exhaustive or random search strategies.
proposals.method: We propose a two-component iterative system. First, a GNN encoder processes observed node signals and a current candidate adjacency matrix to produce node embeddings and model activations. Second, a reinforcement learning agent (or a differentiable attention network) uses these activations as state to sequentially propose a small set of candidate edges to 'query.' Each query evaluates the impact of adding/removing an edge on a reconstruction or predictive loss. The agent is trained with a reward shaping that balances information gain (reduction in loss) against query cost, using policy gradient methods. This process iteratively refines the candidate graph.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, scalable framework for inverse graph learning that merges principles of cognitive attention with structural inference. It will provide a new benchmark for sample-efficient graph topology discovery. The method also offers a step towards more interpretable and data-efficient relational reasoning for complex systems where the underlying graph is unknown.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in framing graph inference as a sequential, attention-guided query problem, directly adapting concepts from visual attention and active learning to the discrete, combinatorial space of graph structures, which is a non-obvious synergy given existing work in self-supervised representation learning and world models.

proposals.title: Cognitive Attention-Guided Search: Integrating Saliency and Reinforcement Learning for Efficient Visual World Models
proposals.motivation: Current self-supervised visual representation learning, particularly in world model architectures, often relies on exhaustive, hard-attention mechanisms like sliding windows for search or prediction. This is computationally wasteful and biologically implausible, failing to mimic the efficient, foveated attention of biological vision. Integrating principles from established computational models of visual attention could lead to more efficient and robust learning systems, a core challenge in autonomous navigation and perception.
proposals.hypothesis: That a visual world model's efficiency and sample complexity can be significantly improved by guiding its spatial search or feature sampling with a trainable attention mechanism inspired by cognitive saliency models and refined through reinforcement learning, rather than using exhaustive scanning.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stream architecture where a fast, bottom-up saliency network (based on pre-2026 models like DeepGaze or ICF) processes the input image to propose a sparse set of candidate fixation points. A reinforcement learning agent (e.g., a small policy network) learns to sequentially select from these candidates to gather visual patches for a primary world model (e.g., a JEPA-like architecture). The RL agent's reward is based on the predictive or representational utility of the selected patches for the main task, creating a top-down, task-driven attention loop. This system will be trained end-to-end on navigation and video prediction benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, biologically-inspired attention mechanism for world models that reduces computational cost. It will demonstrate how explicit saliency and reinforcement learning can be integrated into self-supervised learning pipelines to create more data-efficient and scalable vision systems, advancing towards autonomous agent learning.
proposals.novelty: While saliency models and RL exist, their tight integration into a world model's active perception loop for guiding non-exhaustive search is novel. This moves beyond using attention as just a neural network module (e.g., transformers) to an explicit, controllable policy for computational resource allocation.

proposals.title: Efficient Frontier Learning: Dynamic Multi-Task Loss Balancing via Gradient Covariance Optimization
proposals.motivation: Multi-task learning (MTL) is crucial for developing efficient, generalist AI systems, but performance is highly sensitive to the relative weighting of task losses. Current methods, like uncertainty weighting or gradient normalization, often lack a principled, dynamic framework for managing the complex, training-time interactions between tasks. A systematic theory for balancing competing gradient signals to maximize collective progress on a shared representation is a significant open problem in pre-2026 MTL research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that treating task gradients as assets in a portfolio and applying the risk-return optimization framework of Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT) will yield a dynamic loss-weighting strategy that finds an 'efficient frontier' for multi-task networks, minimizing harmful interference while maximizing overall learning progress.
proposals.method: We will adapt MPT by defining the 'return' of a task as its loss reduction over a training window and the 'risk' as the variance and covariance of task gradient directions. During training, we will maintain a running estimate of the gradient covariance matrix for all tasks. At each optimization step, we will solve for the set of loss weights that maximizes the expected 'return' (aggregate loss decrease) for a target level of 'risk' (gradient conflict), effectively projecting gradients onto an efficient frontier. This will be implemented within a standard multi-task architecture (e.g., a shared encoder with task-specific heads) and evaluated on established multi-modal benchmarks combining classification and regression tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, finance-inspired optimization perspective to MTL, providing a rigorous, online algorithm for dynamic loss balancing. It will advance the field of representation learning by offering a method to more systematically manage competition and synergy between tasks during joint training, moving beyond heuristic weighting schemes.
proposals.novelty: While covariance-aware gradient methods exist, directly mapping the portfolio optimization problem—with its explicit risk-return trade-off and efficient frontier—onto multi-task loss balancing is a novel conceptual and technical crossover not explored in pre-2026 machine learning literature.

proposals.title: Unified Character-Level World Models: Multi-Task Equilibrium for Cross-Script Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Current multilingual models often produce representations biased towards high-resource scripts (e.g., Latin, Cyrillic), failing to generalize well to the long tail of the world's writing systems. This limits the development of truly language-agnostic AI and hinders applications like low-resource machine translation and semantic search. A principled method to balance learning signals across hundreds of scripts is needed to build equitable and robust universal text representations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framing script and language family learning as distinct tasks within a single multi-task framework, and applying dynamic loss-balancing algorithms, will yield character-level representations that are more balanced, information-rich, and transferable across linguistic boundaries than those from standard joint training or script-specific models.
proposals.method: We will train a single Transformer-based encoder on a massive, character-tokenized corpus spanning hundreds of scripts. Each script or language family defines a task, such as masked character modeling, script identification, or cross-script alignment via a shared latent space. We will implement and compare advanced loss-balancing strategies, including Uncertainty Weighting (Kendall et al., 2018) and GradNorm (Chen et al., 2018), to dynamically adjust task weights throughout training. The model's core will be a shared representation layer, with lightweight task-specific heads, optimized by the balanced multi-task objective.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel framework and a publicly released model for learning script-invariant character representations. It will provide empirical evidence on the efficacy of modern multi-task balancing theories for mitigating linguistic bias. The resulting features are expected to significantly improve zero-shot cross-lingual transfer performance on tasks like named entity recognition and text classification for low-resource languages.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit treatment of each script's learning signal as a separate task to be balanced, moving beyond simple mixed-batch training. This formal multi-task perspective, combined with state-of-the-art loss-balancing from computer vision, is a new approach to the core challenge of equitable multilingual representation learning.

proposals.title: Gradient-Based Task Balancing for Self-Supervised World Models
proposals.motivation: Modern self-supervised and world model learning, as explored in LeCun's recent work, inherently involves multiple objectives (e.g., reconstruction, temporal coherence, semantic prediction). Manually balancing these loss terms is suboptimal and limits the efficiency of learning rich, shared representations. Formalizing this as a multi-task learning problem can provide a principled, automated solution to optimize the joint training dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying theoretical insights from multi-task learning—specifically, analyzing the geometry of task gradients—to the multi-objective losses in self-supervised world models will yield an automatic balancing mechanism that outperforms static weighting, leading to more robust and generalizable visual representations.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend gradient-based multi-task balancing algorithms, such as GradNorm or uncertainty weighting, to the context of joint embedding predictive architectures (JEPA) and other world models. The core method will involve dynamically adjusting task loss weights during training based on the magnitude and alignment of their gradients with respect to shared parameters. This will be integrated into a training framework for visual representation learning, evaluating on benchmarks combining classification, regression, and reconstruction tasks from a single visual stream.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, theoretically-grounded method for automatic loss balancing in complex self-supervised systems. It will advance the practical training of world models by removing a key hyperparameter tuning bottleneck and could yield new insights into the interaction of different self-supervised objectives during representation formation.
proposals.novelty: While multi-task balancing is studied in supervised settings, its direct application to the specific, often conflicting objectives within self-supervised world model learning is novel. The proposal bridges the formal theory of multi-task optimization with the practical challenges of modern representation learning pipelines championed by LeCun.

proposals.title: OverFeat-Chiplets: A Modular, Heterogeneous Optimization Framework for Efficient Embedded Vision
proposals.motivation: Deploying accurate convolutional neural networks like OverFeat on mobile and embedded devices is hindered by their monolithic computational and memory demands. While pruning and quantization exist, they often apply uniform strategies, missing opportunities for layer-specific optimization. Drawing an analogy to modern chiplet-based processor design, where specialized cores are optimized independently and integrated via an efficient fabric, could unlock new efficiency frontiers for vision models.
proposals.hypothesis: By decomposing a monolithic CNN into functionally distinct modules and applying a tailored, heterogeneous optimization strategy to each 'neural chiplet,' we can achieve superior efficiency-accuracy trade-offs compared to globally uniform compression methods.
proposals.method: We will first develop an automated analysis to segment OverFeat into modules based on feature hierarchy and sensitivity (e.g., early convolutions, mid-level extractors, classifier). Each module undergoes a specialized process: aggressive 4-bit quantization for early layers, feature-map distillation for intermediate layers using a self-supervised objective, and structured pruning for final fully-connected layers. We will then design and train lightweight, learned connection layers to reintegrate these heterogeneous modules, mimicking a high-bandwidth, low-power on-chip interconnect.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a novel, automated framework for producing highly efficient, heterogeneous vision models and a new state-of-the-art efficient variant of OverFeat for embedded platforms. It contributes a principled methodology, inspired by hardware design, for co-optimizing different network components, advancing the field of efficient deep learning.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the systematic translation of chiplet-style modular, heterogeneous design principles into a neural network optimization framework, moving beyond layer-wise to module-wise specialization and learned inter-module integration.

proposals.title: OverFeat-Lite: A Framework for Efficient, Real-Time Visual Feature Learning on Mobile Devices
proposals.motivation: The original OverFeat framework was pivotal for integrated feature extraction, localization, and detection, but its computational footprint limits deployment on mobile and embedded systems. As of 2025, there is a growing need for efficient, general-purpose vision models that can operate in real-time under strict power and latency constraints, enabling advanced perception for robotics, AR, and IoT devices. This work addresses the gap between powerful convolutional architectures and the practical requirements of edge computing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by applying a structured co-design of architectural scaling, task-driven pruning, and post-training quantization, the core multi-task learning principles of OverFeat can be preserved while achieving an order-of-magnitude reduction in computational cost, enabling real-time performance on mobile hardware without significant accuracy loss on standard vision tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a scaled-down OverFeat-Lite architecture by first reducing filter counts and network depth based on a sensitivity analysis. We will then apply structured network pruning guided by the Hessian of the loss, focusing on filters with low task-specific importance across localization and detection. The pruned model will be trained using knowledge distillation from the full OverFeat model. Finally, we will implement 8-bit integer quantization (INT8) for both weights and activations, leveraging pre-2026 frameworks like TensorFlow Lite, and validate the pipeline on embedded GPU platforms (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson Nano).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a publicly available, optimized OverFeat-Lite model and an associated training framework for efficient multi-task vision. It will provide a detailed empirical analysis of the trade-offs between accuracy, speed, and model size for classical CNN architectures on edge devices. The results will serve as a practical blueprint for deploying legacy but effective vision systems in resource-constrained environments.
proposals.novelty: While network compression is well-studied, a holistic, task-aware compression of the integrated OverFeat pipeline—which jointly learns representations for classification, localization, and detection—for embedded systems is novel. The work uniquely focuses on preserving this multi-task capability during aggressive scaling, a challenge not addressed by compressing single-task classification models like AlexNet or VGG.

proposals.title: Ecological Scaling Laws for Unified Convolutional Networks: A Niche-Based Framework for Predicting Performance from Dataset Size and Diversity
proposals.motivation: The scaling laws for large language models are well-studied, but analogous principles for unified, multi-task convolutional networks (ConvNets) like the OverFeat framework remain poorly understood. As we move towards more general visual systems, we lack a predictive theory for how performance, efficiency, and transferability scale with the size and compositional diversity of training data, hindering efficient model and dataset design.
proposals.hypothesis: The performance scaling of a unified ConvNet can be modeled by ecological niche theory, where performance metrics (accuracy, parameter efficiency) follow predictable power-law relationships as functions of dataset 'niche' dimensions: size, class similarity (phylogenetic distance), and feature variance (environmental gradient).
proposals.method: We will systematically train the OverFeat architecture on nested subsets from large-scale datasets (ImageNet-22k, iNaturalist, DomainNet), where subsets are constructed by sampling along controlled gradients of class semantic similarity (using WordNet hierarchies) and visual feature variance. We will fit scaling laws using a modified species distribution modeling (SDM) framework, treating each output class as a 'species' and modeling its accuracy as a function of dataset 'biome' properties. The analysis will quantify scaling exponents for in-domain accuracy, cross-dataset transfer, and parameter efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, ecology-inspired scaling laws for unified ConvNets, offering a principled framework to predict the data requirements for target performance levels. It will yield practical guidelines for constructing cost-effective, multi-purpose visual systems and a novel theoretical lens for understanding representation learning through the analogy of ecological niche filling.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in applying formal ecological niche modeling and biodiversity scaling theory to the analysis of artificial neural networks, moving beyond simple dataset-size scaling to model the explicit role of semantic and visual diversity.

proposals.title: Scaling Laws for On-Device Graph Foundation Models: Co-Designing Efficiency and Representation Learning on Massive Heterogeneous Graphs
proposals.motivation: The deployment of graph neural networks (GNNs) on resource-constrained edge and IoT devices is hindered by the high computational cost of conventional models, which are trained first and compressed later, often losing robustness. Simultaneously, the success of self-supervised learning in vision and language suggests that scaling data diversity and volume is key to learning generalizable representations. This project asks if we can discover efficient, robust graph foundation models by directly scaling learning under a strict on-device compute budget.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by co-designing a unified, OverFeat-inspired convolutional graph architecture with extreme efficiency constraints (e.g., binarization, pruning) from the outset and training it on massive, heterogeneous graph datasets, we can induce scaling laws where model performance and robustness emerge directly as a function of data scale and diversity, not parameter count, yielding inherently deployable foundation models.
proposals.method: We will develop a unified, multi-scale convolutional architecture for graphs, inspired by OverFeat's efficiency, and apply it to web-scale knowledge graphs (e.g., Wikidata) and temporal sensor networks. From the initial training phase, we will integrate aggressive weight binarization, structured pruning, and low-rank factorizations as intrinsic components of the learning objective, not post-hoc steps. The model will be trained using a multi-task, self-supervised objective combining masked node/edge prediction and contrastive semantic alignment across graph modalities, scaling data volume and heterogeneity systematically to measure emergent properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish empirical scaling laws for graph representation learning under hard efficiency constraints, providing a blueprint for creating natively efficient foundation models. It will deliver a publicly available, lightweight model capable of robust inference on edge devices, challenging the dominant 'train-then-compress' paradigm in graph ML.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious core is the deliberate co-design of scaling and extreme efficiency ab initio, forcing the discovery of maximally informative yet minimal representations directly from large-scale data, rather than treating efficiency as an afterthought. This inverts the standard scaling paradigm for graphs.

proposals.title: Inverse Tomography for Visual Synthesis: Reconstructing Images from Discriminative Feature Projections
proposals.motivation: Current generative models, like GANs and diffusion models, learn a direct mapping from latent space to pixels, often requiring massive data and lacking fine-grained semantic control. Conversely, powerful discriminative models, such as object detectors, learn rich, semantically structured representations but are typically used only for analysis. This project addresses the gap by treating a pre-trained detector as a fixed forward model, enabling precise, representation-driven image synthesis and editing by solving an inverse problem, analogous to reconstructing a CT scan from its projections.
proposals.hypothesis: The internal feature representations of a fixed, pre-trained object detector contain sufficient information to act as a robust, multi-view 'probe', such that an inverse optimization process can reconstruct or edit images with high semantic fidelity and spatial consistency.
proposals.method: We will formalize a pre-trained detector (e.g., a Faster R-CNN or DETR variant) as a non-linear forward operator Φ(I) = (F, B), mapping an image I to intermediate feature maps F and bounding box predictions B. The core algorithm will be an iterative gradient-based inversion, minimizing a loss L = ||Φ(G(z)) - (F_target, B_target)|| + λ R(z), where G is a neural renderer (initially a Deep Image Prior network or a pre-trained generator) and R is a regularization prior. We will adapt techniques from classical tomography (e.g., filtered backprojection concepts) and modern neural field representations to stabilize the ill-posed inversion.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for using discriminative models as 'world probes' for generative tasks, enabling semantically grounded image editing (object insertion, removal, attribute change) without re-training generative models on new concepts. It will provide a novel test for the completeness of learned representations and contribute to the unification of discriminative and generative world models.
proposals.novelty: While feature inversion and GAN priors exist, framing a detector as a tomographic projector and systematically inverting its full output (features and boxes) for generative control is a novel analogy. This approach explicitly leverages the structured, multi-task output of modern detectors, unlike prior work focusing on inverting classifiers or single feature layers.

proposals.title: Inverting Graph Representations: A Theoretical Framework for Stable and Generalizable Generation via Discriminative Features
proposals.motivation: Graph-structured data is ubiquitous, from molecules to social networks, yet generative models for graphs often lack the theoretical grounding and semantic control found in discriminative tasks. Pre-2026, self-supervised learning has excelled at learning rich, semantic representations from images and graphs, but the inverse problem—systematically generating or editing graphs from these learned features—remains poorly understood. This gap limits our ability to perform controllable, theory-backed generation on non-Euclidean domains, a critical step for applications like drug design or network analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: The feature representations learned by a discriminative Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) for a downstream task (e.g., property prediction) encode sufficient topological and semantic information to define a stable and generalizable inverse mapping for graph generation and editing.
proposals.method: We will develop a theoretical framework analyzing the inverse mapping from the latent space of a pre-trained GCN back to the graph domain. The approach will leverage tools from spectral graph theory and perturbation analysis to formally characterize the stability of this inverse to input noise and feature variations. We will instantiate the theory using a multi-task GCN encoder, trained on a combination of supervised and self-supervised objectives (e.g., node classification and graph contrastive learning), and study inversion via gradient-based optimization in the input graph space. Experiments will quantify approximation capabilities and generalization across graph topologies on molecular and point cloud datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal analysis of inverse generation from discriminative graph features, establishing theoretical guarantees on stability and generalization. It will bridge the generative potential of representation learning models with rigorous GCN analysis, enabling more controllable and semantically consistent graph editing. The framework will offer a new, theory-backed avenue for generation on non-Euclidean data, extending principles from LeCun's world model and self-supervised learning 'cookbook' to the graph domain.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses GCNs for generation (e.g., via VAEs or GANs), a direct theoretical analysis of the inverse mapping from a discriminative model's features is novel. The focus on formal properties like stability and generalization across topologies, rather than just empirical performance, represents a significant departure from existing graph generative methods.

proposals.title: Inverting Discriminative World Models: Semantic Image Editing via Object Detector Features
proposals.motivation: Current image generation and editing models, such as GANs and diffusion models, are primarily trained as generative models from the outset. However, powerful self-supervised and multi-task learning methods have created rich, semantic visual representations within discriminative models, like object detectors, that encode world knowledge. The inverse problem of using these frozen, task-oriented features for controlled generation remains largely unexplored, offering a path to more interpretable and semantically-grounded image synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: The feature representations and bounding box predictors of a discriminative world model, trained for detection and recognition, contain sufficient structured semantic and geometric information to drive the generation, removal, or alteration of objects in images in a controllable and consistent manner.
proposals.method: We will leverage a pre-trained, self-supervised vision transformer (e.g., DINOv2) or a multi-task model with object detection heads as a frozen, semantic feature backbone. An adversarial or diffusion-based decoder will be trained to invert these features, conditioned on bounding box parameters and class labels. The training objective will combine reconstruction loss on real images with a consistency loss ensuring the generated images, when fed back into the frozen detector, produce the target detections. We will explore iterative refinement using the detector's gradient to guide the generation process.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a novel paradigm for image editing that directly leverages the semantic understanding of discriminative models, potentially leading to more precise and interpretable control. It will provide new insights into the generative capabilities latent within features learned for recognition, bridging the gap between discriminative and generative world models as outlined in LeCun's research agenda.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses GANs or diffusion models for editing, our approach uniquely inverts a frozen, pre-trained object detector—a purely discriminative model—for generation, treating its features as a semantic constraint engine rather than training an end-to-end generative model from scratch.

proposals.title: A Sparsely-Gated Mixture of Experts for Multi-Task Visual Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Current multi-task learning (MTL) systems suffer from catastrophic interference and negative transfer, where learning one task degrades performance on another, limiting their scalability to the diverse set of tasks required for robust machine perception. This is a critical bottleneck for developing unified, efficient world models for autonomous systems, a core challenge in pre-2026 self-supervised and representation learning research. A method that enables positive transfer between related tasks while isolating conflicting ones is needed to move beyond task-specific silos.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single, shared feature pyramid, modulated by task-conditioned, sparsely-gated binary routing masks, can learn a rich common representation while dynamically isolating task-specific computations, thereby minimizing gradient conflicts and enabling scalable, positive transfer across a large set of visual tasks.
proposals.method: We will build upon a shared backbone (e.g., a ResNet or Vision Transformer) producing a multi-scale feature pyramid. For each task, a lightweight, conditionally-activated router network (a small MLP) will take a task embedding as input and output a sparse, binary mask per feature level, selecting a subset of channels to route to that task's head. The routing will be trained with a straight-through Gumbel-Softmax estimator to enable gradient flow. We will train the system jointly on a diverse set of visual tasks (e.g., classification, detection, depth, surface normals, semantic segmentation) from datasets like Taskonomy and COCO.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a scalable and parameter-efficient architecture for unified multi-task visual learning that demonstrably reduces catastrophic interference. It will provide empirical evidence and a practical framework for building more capable and general visual perception systems, advancing towards the goal of learning comprehensive world models. The proposed router mechanism offers a concrete path to dynamic network specialization within a shared parameter pool.
proposals.novelty: While Mixture of Experts (MoE) models and task-conditioning exist, applying a sparsely-gated, binary feature-routing mechanism specifically to solve gradient conflict in dense visual MTL is novel. This approach differs from prior soft-attention or weighted-sharing methods by enforcing hard, task-adaptive sparsity in the feature flow, a non-obvious but biologically-plausible strategy for interference minimization.

proposals.title: A Software-Defined Vision Network: Scaling Multi-Task Learning with Dynamic Task Modulation Heads
proposals.motivation: Current multi-task networks are typically designed for a small, fixed set of tasks, limiting their scalability and adaptability. This is inefficient, as each new task often requires training a new model from scratch or fine-tuning a large backbone, wasting computational resources and failing to leverage a truly shared, generic visual representation. A more flexible, scalable paradigm is needed to enable a single visual system to efficiently master a broad and evolving set of perceptual tasks, moving towards more general-purpose visual intelligence.
proposals.hypothesis: A single, shared visual feature backbone, when conditioned by lightweight, task-specific modulation heads, can efficiently and effectively solve a diverse and scalable set of visual tasks without catastrophic interference or the need for full retraining. The performance on this broad task set will be competitive with specialized single-task models while being far more parameter- and data-efficient.
proposals.method: We will develop a unified architecture with a large, self-supervised pre-trained backbone (e.g., a Vision Transformer or ConvNet pyramid) as the common feature extractor. For each new task (e.g., depth, normals, segmentation), we will attach a small, plug-and-play 'Task Modulation Head.' These heads will not process features directly but will instead generate parameters (e.g., scaling and shifting vectors) for feature-wise linear modulation (FiLM) or adaptive instance normalization (AdaIN) layers that condition the activations of the shared backbone. A multi-task training regime will be used, potentially with gradient modulation techniques to manage task interference, and the framework will be evaluated on a broad benchmark like Taskonomy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, scalable architecture for general-purpose visual representation learning, demonstrating that a single network can be dynamically configured for many tasks. It will deliver a practical framework for efficiently adding new capabilities to a visual system, reducing the need for redundant model storage and training. The results will advance the field towards more modular, composable, and efficient multi-task learning systems.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the explicit adaptation of the Software-Defined Radio paradigm to computer vision, framing task-specific heads as lightweight 'modulators' of a shared signal rather than heavy 'processors,' enabling dynamic task scalability. This goes beyond prior multi-task learning by focusing on extreme scalability and the plug-and-play addition of tasks without redesigning the core network.

proposals.title: A Unified Multi-Task Visual World Model: Scaling Shared Representations to Dense and Geometric Tasks
