proposals.motivation: Current multi-task networks primarily combine high-level semantic tasks like classification and detection, leaving a gap in integrating low-level and geometric tasks (e.g., depth, normals) that are crucial for a comprehensive visual world model. Scaling the number and diversity of tasks is essential for advancing self-supervised learning and building more general, data-efficient AI systems that understand the 3D structure of the world, a core pursuit in pre-2026 representation learning research.
proposals.hypothesis: A single neural network, built upon a shared multi-scale feature pyramid, can learn a unified visual representation that effectively supports simultaneous training and inference on a broad spectrum of 10+ tasks spanning semantic, geometric, and structural domains without catastrophic interference, leading to richer and more robust features.
proposals.method: We will extend a backbone feature pyramid network (e.g., a Swin Transformer or ConvNeXt) with a unified task head architecture. Each task-specific decoder (for classification, bounding boxes, dense depth, surface normals, keypoints, etc.) will be a lightweight, task-conditioned adapter module that queries and fuses features from the shared pyramid. We will employ a gradient balancing strategy (e.g., uncertainty weighting or GradNorm) during multi-task optimization and leverage a large-scale, multi-annotated dataset (e.g., a curated subset of Taskonomy or a newly synthesized dataset) for joint training.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete architecture and training recipe for scaling visual multi-task learning to an unprecedented breadth of tasks, directly contributing to the 'cookbook' of self-supervised and multi-task learning. It will empirically demonstrate the synergies and interference patterns between diverse visual tasks, offering insights for building more general-purpose visual world models.
proposals.novelty: The systematic scaling to include dense prediction and geometric tasks within a single unified network, moving beyond the common triad of classification, detection, and segmentation, represents a significant and non-trivial expansion of the multi-task paradigm in computer vision.

proposals.title: Variable-Length Input Processing via Adaptive Character-Level Convolutional Architectures
proposals.motivation: Current deep learning models for text, particularly those based on Transformers, impose a fixed maximum sequence length, forcing truncation of long documents or wasteful padding of short ones. This constraint limits the faithful processing of long-form documents and is inefficient for real-world data with high length variance. A method for truly variable-length input would enable more flexible and efficient learning from raw text.
proposals.hypothesis: That a character-level convolutional neural network (ConvNet), augmented with an adaptive feature aggregation mechanism, can learn robust semantic representations from text of arbitrary length without a predefined length cap, matching or exceeding the performance of fixed-length models on standard tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a deep ConvNet operating directly on character sequences. To handle variable length, we will replace final global pooling with an adaptive pooling layer that dynamically aggregates convolutional feature maps to a fixed-size representation, regardless of input length. We will explore recurrent connections (e.g., LSTMs) on top of the convolutional features for sequential context. The model will be trained using self-supervised objectives, such as masked character prediction and next-sentence prediction, inspired by world model learning, before fine-tuning on supervised tasks like text classification and sentiment analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical architecture for processing documents of arbitrary length, removing a significant engineering constraint in NLP. It will contribute to the field of self-supervised representation learning by demonstrating how ConvNets, with their strong inductive biases for local structure, can be effectively scaled to variable-length sequences. The resulting model could serve as a more computationally efficient alternative to Transformers for certain long-document tasks.
proposals.novelty: While character-level ConvNets exist, they typically use fixed-size inputs or simple pooling. The novelty lies in the systematic integration of adaptive pooling and recurrent layers within a ConvNet framework specifically designed for end-to-end, variable-length self-supervised learning, moving beyond the fixed-context windows of contemporary BERT-style models.

proposals.title: Global Character Order Parameters: A Mean-Field Architecture for Self-Supervised Language Modeling
proposals.motivation: Current self-supervised learning for language, even at the character level, builds representations from local token or n-gram interactions, expecting long-range coherence to emerge. This is analogous to explaining superconductivity from local spin sums alone. We hypothesize that explicitly modeling global character statistics as a primary architectural constraint, inspired by mean-field theory in statistical mechanics, can lead to more sample-efficient learning of linguistic structure and better generalization in low-resource or multi-lingual settings.
proposals.hypothesis: Treating global character co-occurrence patterns and positional harmonics as explicit 'order parameters' that guide local feature extraction will yield more coherent, data-efficient character-level representations than architectures where long-range dependencies are only emergent.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage encoder architecture. First, a lightweight 'Global Constraint Module' processes the full input character sequence to compute primary order parameters: a regularized character co-occurrence matrix and a set of learned positional spectral embeddings via a short Fourier Neural Network. Second, these global parameters are used to condition a local feature extractor (e.g., a stack of gated convolutional or local attention layers) via feature-wise linear modulation (FiLM). The entire system will be trained end-to-end using a multi-task self-supervised objective combining masked character prediction and a contrastive task based on the global coherence of perturbed sequences.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel architectural paradigm for representation learning that inverts the standard local-to-global hierarchy. It provides a concrete framework for integrating statistical mechanics analogies into deep learning design. We expect to demonstrate improved sample efficiency and robustness on character-level tasks like text classification, unsupervised segmentation, and low-resource language modeling.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the explicit treatment of global character statistics as a primary, learnable architectural constraint that modulates local processing, directly inspired by mean-field theory. This contrasts with all existing approaches where such statistics are implicit outcomes of deep, autoregressive, or self-attentive stacks.

proposals.title: World-Model-Guided Adaptive Perception: A Global-to-Local Architecture for Efficient Visual Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Current vision architectures, including standard CNNs and hybrid models, often process visual inputs with dense, uniform sampling, which is computationally wasteful. A fundamental goal in autonomous learning systems, as explored in LeCun's world model research, is to achieve human-like efficiency by focusing computational resources on contextually salient regions. This project addresses the critical need for architectures that dynamically allocate computation based on a task-relevant global understanding, moving beyond static, data-agnostic processing.
proposals.hypothesis: That a primary, lightweight global context module—modeled as a latent world state—can accurately predict a sparse, adaptive spatial sampling mask, enabling a secondary local feature extractor to achieve superior computational efficiency and richer semantic representations compared to dense or uniformly sparse baselines.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stream architecture. First, a lightweight Transformer or a non-parametric self-attention module processes a heavily downsampled version of the input to build a global context representation and a latent task embedding. This representation conditions a Spatial Sampling Predictor, a small network that outputs a probabilistic saliency map over spatial locations and scales. A Gumbel-Softmax or reinforcement learning-based selector then samples a sparse set of image patches, which are processed at high resolution by a secondary convolutional network. The entire system will be trained end-to-end using a multi-task objective combining a primary task (e.g., image classification, navigation) with auxiliary losses encouraging sparsity and reconstruction of the global context.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, invertible architecture paradigm where global context primacy directs local computation, directly advancing the field of efficient self-supervised and task-driven representation learning. It will provide a concrete framework for integrating world model concepts into perceptual systems, yielding measurable gains in compute-per-pixel efficiency. The resulting adaptive sampling mechanism could be a core component for next-generation autonomous systems requiring real-time perception.
proposals.novelty: While adaptive computation and sparse attention exist, this work inverts the standard convolutional priority by making detailed local extraction explicitly subservient to a learned global world model, creating a top-down, goal-directed perceptual loop. This synthesis of world model guidance with adaptive spatial sampling for general representation learning is a distinct departure from existing hybrid architectures.

proposals.title: Character-Level JEPA: Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures for Global Character Dependencies
proposals.motivation: Current self-supervised learning for text, especially at the character level, heavily relies on local convolutional or n-gram masking objectives, which may prematurely constrain learned representations. This limits the model's ability to capture long-range semantic and syntactic structures from the raw character stream, a fundamental capability for robust language understanding and generation. A method that treats global dependencies as primary from the start could lead to more data-efficient and compositionally generalizable representations.
proposals.hypothesis: By inverting the typical learning priority to model global character co-occurrence and positional patterns first, a Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) can learn richer, more semantically structured character-level representations than models that begin with strong local feature priors.
proposals.method: We will design a character-level JEPA where the encoder operates on the full character sequence, using a Transformer with relative positional encodings to inherently capture long-range dependencies. The predictor will be tasked not with reconstructing masked local patches, but with predicting the latent representation of a distant character segment from the representation of a context segment, enforcing a global coherence objective. This will be trained using a contrastive or regression-based loss in the latent space, as outlined in LeCun's JEPA framework, avoiding generative pixel/character-level reconstruction.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel self-supervised objective and architecture for character-level representation learning that prioritizes global structure. It will provide empirical evidence on whether such an inversion leads to improved performance on downstream tasks like text classification, named entity recognition, and morphological analysis compared to local-first baselines.
proposals.novelty: While Transformers are used for characters, their pre-training typically still uses local token masking (e.g., BERT-style). The novelty lies in explicitly formulating a non-generative, predictive world model objective (JEPA) at the character level that treats global dependencies as the primary learning signal, not a supplement.

proposals.title: Dynamic Subword Gating: Learning Adaptive Token Representations via Contextual Granularity Selection
proposals.motivation: Current language models rely on fixed tokenization schemes (e.g., subword units like BPE), which are inefficient. They treat all tokens uniformly, wasting computation on common words and losing robustness on rare or noisy text. A more efficient and adaptive model should learn to dynamically choose the optimal representational granularity—character or word—based on local context.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid character-word model with a lightweight, learned gating function can dynamically select the most efficient and effective representational pathway per token, leading to superior parameter efficiency and robustness compared to static architectures.
proposals.method: We will design a transformer-based architecture with parallel character-level (CNN or shallow Transformer over character n-grams) and word-level (pre-trained embedding table) pathways. A small, feed-forward gating network will process the local character sequence to produce a soft selection weight. This weight will interpolate between the character-composed representation and the retrieved word vector, with the entire system trained end-to-end via a masked token prediction or downstream task objective. The gating function will be regularized to encourage discrete decisions for efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, parameter-efficient language model architecture that adapts its computational strategy to input statistics. It will provide a framework for learning multi-granularity world models of language, advancing the principles of self-supervised representation learning. The results will demonstrate measurable gains in inference speed and robustness to out-of-vocabulary terms.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior hybrid models with static dual pathways, our model learns a dynamic, token-wise gating policy. This moves beyond simple interpolation to a context-aware routing mechanism, directly linking the learning of representational granularity to local semantic and morphological cues.

proposals.title: Efficient Hierarchical Convolutional Architectures for Scalable Text Representation on Large Alphabets and Long Sequences
proposals.motivation: Current self-supervised representation learning for text, as exemplified by methods like wav2vec 2.0 for speech or character-level models, often operates on constrained alphabets and sequence lengths. This limits their applicability to real-world text data, which features rich Unicode character sets (e.g., multi-lingual text, code, mathematical notation) and document-scale contexts. Efficiently scaling to these regimes is crucial for building more general and capable AI systems that can learn directly from raw textual signals without heavy tokenization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical, multi-scale convolutional architecture with adaptive pooling and dilated convolutions can learn semantically rich representations from raw character sequences with large alphabets and extreme lengths, while maintaining computational efficiency comparable to subword tokenization approaches.
proposals.method: We will extend the core convolutional framework from prior work (e.g., wav2vec-style encoders) by designing a hierarchical encoder. The first layer will use a learned embedding for a large Unicode-aware alphabet (~50k+ characters) followed by a strided temporal convolution for initial downsampling. Subsequent blocks will employ dilated convolutions with exponentially increasing receptive fields and adaptive pooling layers to compress sequence length progressively. We will train this model using a self-supervised contrastive or masked prediction task on a multi-lingual corpus, evaluating representation quality on downstream tasks like language identification, semantic similarity, and long-range dependency modeling.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable, convolution-based architecture blueprint for raw text representation learning that bypasses the need for fixed vocabularies. It will demonstrate empirically that efficient, tokenizer-free models can handle diverse and long-form text, contributing a new tool to the self-supervised learning cookbook for modalities beyond images and speech.
proposals.novelty: While hierarchical and dilated convolutions are known, their systematic integration and optimization for the specific challenges of massive-alphabet, long-sequence raw text representation—moving beyond the small-alphabet regimes of prior character-level work—is novel and non-trivial.

proposals.title: Learning Multi-Scale World Models for Text: Dynamic Dilated Convolutions with Context-Aware Gating
proposals.motivation: Character-level models must capture hierarchical linguistic structures, from local morphemes to long-range dependencies, within a single, efficient architecture. Fixed-scale or rigidly scheduled dilated convolutions, while parameter-efficient, are insufficiently adaptive to the variable context required for robust language understanding and generation. This limits their ability to learn the kind of rich, multi-scale world models for language that are central to advancing self-supervised representation learning.
proposals.hypothesis: A convolutional network with a learned, input-adaptive dilation gating mechanism will more effectively capture the multi-scale nature of language than models with fixed dilation schedules, leading to superior character-level representations for downstream tasks.
proposals.method: We propose a novel convolutional block where multiple parallel dilated convolutional branches operate on the same character embedding input, each with a different dilation rate. A lightweight, learned gating network—implemented as a small MLP or 1D convolution—processes the local context to produce a softmax-weighted blend of the branch outputs. This gating mechanism is trained end-to-end via a self-supervised objective, such as masked character prediction, allowing the model to dynamically select receptive fields based on the immediate linguistic context.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, adaptive architecture for character-level modeling that combines the efficiency of convolutions with the flexibility of dynamic structure. It will provide a concrete method for learning multi-scale representations of text, advancing the field of self-supervised learning for language. The results will demonstrate the value of input-conditional computation in convolutional networks for sequence modeling.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the learned, context-dependent gating mechanism that dynamically blends fixed dilation rates, moving beyond static or scheduled dilation patterns. This integrates a conditional computation paradigm directly into the dilated convolution framework for adaptive multi-scale modeling.

proposals.title: Virtual Source Convolutions: A Seismic Interferometry-Inspired Block for Efficient Long-Range Character Modeling
proposals.motivation: Character-level ConvNets offer robust, language-agnostic text processing but struggle to capture long-range dependencies without deep, computationally expensive stacks. Pre-2026 self-supervised learning emphasizes learning rich, structured world models from data, yet efficient architectural priors for character sequences remain under-explored. This limits the ability to model multi-word expressions or syntactic clauses directly from raw character inputs in a parameter-efficient manner.
proposals.hypothesis: A convolutional block that explicitly couples a standard convolution (physical receiver) with a dilated convolution (virtual receiver) through a cross-correlation-inspired interaction mechanism can synthesize features representing longer-range character relationships, thereby creating a more effective and efficient receptive field than sequential stacking alone.
proposals.method: We design a novel 'Virtual Source Convolution' (VSC) block. It processes a character embedding sequence through two parallel 1D convolutional pathways: a standard convolution with a small kernel (e.g., 3) and a dilated convolution (e.g., dilation rate 3). Inspired by seismic interferometry's virtual source creation, we compute a pairwise feature interaction matrix via a lightweight, learnable cross-correlation operation between the two pathway outputs. This matrix is then processed by a small feed-forward network to generate a 'virtual context' vector, which is fused with the original pathway features via gated summation. Multiple VSC blocks can be stacked or interleaved with standard layers.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new, interpretable convolutional primitive for character-level NLP that explicitly models long-range dependencies with improved parameter efficiency. It will provide a novel architectural bridge between signal processing concepts from geophysics and deep learning for sequences. The resulting models are expected to achieve stronger performance on tasks requiring character-level semantic understanding, such as text classification or named entity recognition, at lower computational cost.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the direct, structured analogy from seismic interferometry's virtual source methodology to a neural network component, moving beyond simple application of dilated convolutions. The designed interaction mechanism for synthesizing a 'virtual' receptive field from two convolutional views is a non-obvious and previously unexplored approach for character modeling.

proposals.title: Learning Hierarchical Character Representations with Dilated Convolutional Networks
proposals.motivation: Character-level convolutional networks (ConvNets) are effective for text understanding but require deep stacks of layers to capture long-range dependencies, increasing computational cost and training difficulty. Dilated convolutions, proven in audio (WaveNet) and image segmentation, efficiently expand the receptive field while preserving resolution and computational efficiency. Applying this technique to character sequences could enable more efficient learning of multi-scale linguistic patterns, advancing representation learning for text.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating dilated convolutions into character-level ConvNets will allow them to capture longer-range character and sub-word patterns more efficiently than standard convolutions, leading to improved performance on semantic tasks with fewer parameters and less depth.
proposals.method: We will design a dilated convolutional architecture for character embeddings, starting with standard convolutions for local patterns and progressively increasing dilation rates in higher layers (e.g., 1, 2, 4, 8). This architecture will be benchmarked against deep standard ConvNets and recurrent baselines on self-supervised tasks like masked character prediction and next-character prediction, using datasets such as Wikipedia. We will analyze the learned representations by evaluating them on downstream tasks like text classification and semantic similarity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, efficient architectural primitive for character-level NLP, reducing model depth and computational cost for long-context modeling. It will contribute to the self-supervised learning 'cookbook' by demonstrating how dilated convolutions can learn hierarchical world models from raw text sequences, advancing the goal of learning general representations from data.
proposals.novelty: While dilated convolutions are established in audio and vision, their systematic application and analysis for learning character-level representations in NLP is novel. The integration of this technique into a self-supervised, multi-task learning framework for semantic representation learning is a non-obvious extension of pre-2026 methods.

proposals.title: Metabolic Bypass Networks: Adaptive Information Shunts for Deep Character-Level Convolutional Models
proposals.motivation: Deep character-level convolutional networks (ConvNets) are powerful for learning hierarchical text representations directly from raw characters, bypassing the need for tokenization. However, they are notoriously difficult to train beyond a certain depth due to severe vanishing gradients and instability, limiting their capacity and performance. While residual connections from ResNets offer a generic solution, they are a static, additive mechanism that may not be optimal for the complex, sequential feature hierarchies in text.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by drawing a formal analogy to engineered metabolic bypass pathways, we can design structured, adaptive information shunts that dynamically route gradients and activations around problematic network segments, enabling the stable training of significantly deeper and more powerful character ConvNets.
proposals.method: We will design a novel 'Metabolic Bypass Block' (MBB) for character ConvNets. Instead of simple additive skip connections, an MBB will incorporate a learned, input-dependent gating mechanism (e.g., via a small auxiliary network or sigmoidal attention) to compute a weighted combination of a direct 'shunt' path and the standard convolutional transformation path. The gating function will be trained to identify and circumvent layers where gradient flow degrades, effectively creating dynamic, learned shortcuts. We will integrate these MBBs into a deep character ConvNet architecture and benchmark against standard ResNet-style baselines on text classification and semantic similarity tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, biologically-inspired architectural primitive for building very deep character-level models, advancing the frontier of tokenization-free NLP. It will provide empirical evidence on the limits of depth in character ConvNets and a principled method to overcome them, potentially leading to state-of-the-art results on tasks requiring long-range character dependencies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving beyond static residual connections by formalizing the metabolic bypass analogy, resulting in a data-dependent, gated routing mechanism specifically tailored to the instability problems of deep character convolutions, a concept not explored in prior NLP or vision work.

proposals.title: Meta-Compressed Character Models: A Self-Supervised Framework for Few-Shot Task Adaptation on Micro-Controllers
proposals.motivation: Deploying adaptable NLP models on micro-controllers is critical for intelligent edge devices, but current approaches are limited. Static, single-task compressed models are brittle, while standard meta-learning methods produce models too large for extreme resource constraints. A new paradigm is needed to co-optimize for sub-100KB model footprints, efficient on-device adaptation, and robust few-shot learning across multiple text tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learning pipeline, trained via self-supervised objectives on a distribution of character-level text tasks, can produce a foundational compressed model whose internal representations enable efficient generation of task-specific parameters via a lightweight, few-shot adaptation step, achieving superior multi-task performance compared to static compression techniques.
proposals.method: We will develop a character-level Transformer encoder as the foundational model. A meta-learning outer loop, using algorithms like MAML or Reptile, will train this model on a broad distribution of self-supervised proxy tasks (e.g., next-character prediction, masked character modeling) to build general representations. The inner loop adaptation will be realized via a small, learned hypernetwork or adapter module that, given a few labeled examples from a new task (e.g., sentiment), generates task-specific final layer parameters or modulation weights. The entire system will be co-optimized using a loss combining reconstruction error, few-shot classification accuracy, and a hardware-aware cost penalizing model size and adaptation latency.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel framework for creating ultra-tiny, inherently adaptable NLP models for the edge, moving beyond static compression. It will provide new insights into how meta-learning and representation learning can be jointly leveraged under extreme model size constraints, with benchmarks for few-shot adaptation of character models on micro-controller hardware.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the tight integration of meta-learning for adaptability with extreme compression targets (<100KB), specifically for character-level models, which is unexplored. Unlike prior work that compresses a large model per task or applies standard meta-learning, this method learns to generate adaptable, compressed representations from the outset.

proposals.title: Synthesizing Minimal Functional Architectures for Character-Level Models via Neural Genome Search
proposals.motivation: Deploying deep learning models on microcontrollers and edge devices with severe memory and compute constraints remains a significant challenge. Current approaches like pruning and distillation start from large, over-parameterized networks, which is inefficient and may retain unnecessary structural complexity. A more fundamental, first-principles approach to model design is needed for extreme-scale scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: For a specific character-level text classification task and hardware target, a minimal, high-performing neural network can be synthesized from scratch by treating its architectural components as a 'genome' to be optimized, analogous to designing a minimal synthetic genome for life in a constrained environment.
proposals.method: We will formulate a search space defining the 'genetic' elements of a tiny character-level ConvNet: possible filter sizes, layer depths, pooling operations, and embedding dimensions. A hardware-in-the-loop evolutionary algorithm or a differentiable NAS variant will serve as the 'synthesis' engine, directly optimizing for accuracy, memory footprint, and inference latency on the target microcontroller. The search objective is to find the simplest architecture meeting a pre-defined performance threshold, effectively identifying only the essential components.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a methodology for creating ultra-compact, task-specific models synthesized for efficiency from inception, moving beyond pruning-based paradigms. It will yield new insights into the minimal architectural requirements for fundamental NLP tasks on edge devices, contributing to the field of efficient deep learning.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the conceptual shift from reduction (pruning) to synthesis, inspired by minimal genome design in synthetic biology. Unlike standard NAS which often seeks peak performance, our method explicitly searches for the minimal architecture satisfying a sufficiency condition, a different and underexplored objective.

proposals.title: Extreme Scale-Down of Character-Level ConvNets via Self-Supervised World Models for Micro-Controller Deployment
proposals.motivation: The deployment of intelligent language models on ubiquitous, resource-constrained micro-controllers (MCUs) is a critical frontier for privacy-preserving, low-latency, and robust IoT applications. However, current model compression techniques often fail at the extreme scale required for MCUs, where memory is measured in kilobytes and compute in megahertz. This work addresses the gap by focusing on character-level ConvNets, which are inherently lightweight but under-explored for such severe constraints.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a self-supervised, multi-task learning objective derived from a learned world model of character sequence dynamics can produce robust, information-rich representations, enabling subsequent extreme compression via quantization and pruning without catastrophic performance loss on downstream text classification tasks.
proposals.method: We will first adapt the character-level ConvNet architecture from prior work for self-supervised learning, using a multi-task objective combining masked character prediction and next-character prediction to learn a compact world model. This pre-trained model will then undergo aggressive iterative magnitude pruning and 4-bit integer quantization, guided by a distillation loss from the original model to preserve semantic information. The final model will be optimized for deployment on ARM Cortex-M series MCUs using TensorFlow Lite Micro.
proposals.contribution: This research will deliver a novel, end-to-end pipeline for creating sub-100KB character-level models that maintain usable accuracy for tasks like sentiment analysis or topic classification on MCUs. It will contribute a new benchmark for evaluating NLP models under extreme hardware constraints and demonstrate the utility of self-supervised world models as a foundation for robust compression.
proposals.novelty: While model compression is well-studied, the novelty lies in the specific fusion of a self-supervised, world-model-based pre-training strategy for character ConvNets with extreme post-training quantization and pruning, targeting the uniquely severe limitations of micro-controllers, an underexplored deployment platform for NLP.

proposals.title: Learning Language-Agnostic Representations via Multi-Granular Visual-Linguistic Character Encoding
proposals.motivation: Current multilingual models rely on large, script-specific tokenizers that are inefficient for low-resource languages and fail to exploit the visual-orthographic regularities shared across scripts. This limits their ability to perform robust cross-lingual transfer, especially for languages with sparse textual data. A method that jointly learns from the visual form and linguistic function of characters could enable more efficient and generalized representation learning.
proposals.hypothesis: That a dual-path architecture, which processes characters as both visual glyphs and discrete linguistic tokens, can learn more language-agnostic and data-efficient representations than purely token-based models by leveraging shared visual patterns across different writing systems.
proposals.method: We propose OmniScript, a model with a dual-path encoder. The visual path uses a lightweight CNN (e.g., a ResNet variant) to process rendered character glyphs into embeddings that capture shape similarities. The linguistic path uses a script-informed subword tokenizer (inspired by SentencePiece) and standard transformer embeddings. A fusion module (e.g., cross-attention or concatenation followed by a projection) combines these representations. The model will be trained with a multi-task self-supervised objective combining masked character modeling and a contrastive loss aligning visual and linguistic embeddings of the same character.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel architecture for cross-lingual learning that reduces vocabulary sparsity and improves sample efficiency, particularly for low-resource scripts. It will provide empirical evidence for the utility of visual information in foundational language model pre-training, advancing the field of multimodal and language-agnostic representation learning.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the explicit, learnable integration of rendered character glyphs as a direct input modality for language model pre-training, moving beyond purely symbolic or pixel-level document image approaches. This multi-granular encoding of characters as both image and token is unexplored in mainstream NLP.

proposals.title: PhyloText: A Unified Character-Level World Model via Phylogenetic Sequence Alignment for Cross-Script Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Current self-supervised learning for text often operates within a single script or uses separate tokenizers, failing to capture deep, language-agnostic patterns across diverse writing systems. This limits the development of truly universal, data-efficient models for multilingual understanding and generation. Drawing inspiration from computational biology, where Multiple Sequence Alignment (MSA) successfully finds conserved patterns across divergent species, offers a powerful analogy for aligning linguistic sequences from different 'evolutionary' branches (scripts).
proposals.hypothesis: That initializing and constraining a single, large-vocabulary character embedding model with a learned 'linguistic substitution matrix'—analogous to BLOSUM/PAM matrices in genomics—will provide a structural prior enabling more effective discovery of cross-script semantic and phonetic correspondences, leading to superior language-agnostic representations.
proposals.method: We will construct a massive multi-script corpus and define a unified alphabet of characters. A trainable substitution matrix will be initialized using pre-2026 knowledge of phonetic features (IPA), visual grapheme topology (via pre-trained vision features), and semantic radical composition. This matrix will score character similarities to guide a novel alignment-augmented transformer or convolutional architecture during self-supervised pre-training, using masked character modeling and next-character prediction tasks across aligned script sequences. The model will be trained end-to-end, allowing the substitution scores and representations to co-evolve.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for unified, character-level multilingual modeling that moves beyond script-specific processing. It will yield a publicly available linguistic substitution matrix quantifying cross-script relationships and demonstrate improved performance on cross-lingual transfer, low-resource language modeling, and potentially script-invariant semantic retrieval tasks.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the direct computational analogy to phylogenetic sequence alignment in genomics, operationalized through a learnable substitution matrix for linguistic characters. This provides a biologically-inspired, structurally grounded prior for cross-script learning, distinct from standard multilingual BERT or script-agnostic byte-level models.

proposals.title: Learning a Unified Visual-Semantic World Model from Multi-Script Character Streams
proposals.motivation: Current self-supervised representation learning excels within single scripts or modalities, but a truly general AI must understand information across diverse human writing systems. Pre-2026, models for Latin script text or single-script image recognition are mature, but a unified model for cross-lingual, cross-script character-level patterns is lacking. This gap limits the development of language-agnostic foundational models for global-scale visual and semantic tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: A single neural model, trained with a joint contrastive and masked character modeling objective on a massive corpus of images and text from multiple scripts (Latin, Cyrillic, Chinese, etc.), can learn a shared, script-invariant representation space that facilitates superior cross-lingual transfer and script-robust classification compared to script-specific or word-level models.
proposals.method: We will construct a unified alphabet of thousands of character/glyph tokens from multiple writing systems. A transformer-based architecture, inspired by the I-JEPA framework for world modeling, will process raw image patches of text and corresponding character sequences. The core training will combine: 1) a masked character modeling loss on the character stream to learn contextual patterns, and 2) a contrastive loss (e.g., InfoNCE) between image embeddings and character sequence embeddings to align visual and semantic information. Training data will be web-scale, multi-lingual image-text pairs (e.g., from Common Crawl).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a scalable recipe for building a single visual-semantic model that operates across scripts, advancing towards a more universal world model. It will provide empirical evidence for the efficiency and robustness of character-level, multi-script learning. The resulting representations and model are expected to set new benchmarks in cross-lingual zero-shot transfer tasks and optical character recognition for low-resource scripts.
proposals.novelty: While multi-lingual BERT exists at the word/subword level, applying a character-level, multi-script approach within a joint visual-semantic world model framework is novel. The key innovation is treating diverse scripts as a unified visual and symbolic system for self-supervised learning, moving beyond the paradigm of separate tokenizers for each language family.

proposals.title: Inverse Feature Synthesis: Generating Text from Learned Character-Level World Models
proposals.motivation: Current self-supervised learning excels at extracting rich, multi-level representations from data, but the inverse process—generating coherent data from these internal representations—remains a significant challenge for understanding what models have truly learned. This is especially true for discrete, sequential data like text, where generative models often operate on token probabilities without direct grounding in the feature spaces of discriminative models. A method to synthesize text from a fixed feature extractor would provide a powerful tool for model interpretability and controllable generation.
proposals.hypothesis: A character-level convolutional network (ConvNet), trained via self-supervision, learns a 'world model' of text where its high-level feature vectors act as a compositional spectral library; a generative decoder can be trained to perform an inverse mapping, synthesizing coherent character sequences from these feature vectors.
proposals.method: We will first train a character-level ConvNet (e.g., a ByteNet or a 1D ResNet) using a self-supervised objective, such as masked character prediction, to serve as a fixed 'spectrograph.' Its penultimate layer activations will define our feature spectra. We will then train a conditional generative model (e.g., a Transformer or LSTM decoder) to reconstruct training text sequences from these feature vectors. The decoder's objective is to generate a character sequence that, when passed through the fixed ConvNet, produces a feature vector close to the conditioning target, implementing a differentiable approximation of the inverse problem via straight-through estimation or REINFORCE.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel paradigm for text generation that is directly grounded in the feature space of a discriminative world model, moving beyond next-token prediction. It will provide a new diagnostic tool for analyzing the semantic and structural information encoded in self-supervised representations. Furthermore, it could enable new forms of controlled generation by manipulating the target feature vector.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in framing text generation as an inverse problem relative to a fixed feature extractor, drawing a direct analogy to spectral synthesis in astrophysics. Unlike standard language models, the generative process is explicitly constrained to produce outputs that are consistent with the internal 'world model' of a separately trained ConvNet.

proposals.title: Joint World Modeling via a Shared Visual Pyramid with Inverse Generative Textual Decoding
proposals.motivation: Current self-supervised and multi-task learning systems excel at building rich visual representations but lack an explicit, interpretable link to language, a core component of human understanding. This gap limits their utility for autonomous systems that must both perceive geometric scene properties (depth, normals) and articulate their reasoning. A unified model that synthesizes textual descriptions directly from a shared multi-task feature space would provide a crucial step towards more grounded and communicative AI.
proposals.hypothesis: A single, scaled network with a shared multi-scale feature pyramid can be augmented with a character-level decoder to generate accurate, descriptive text conditioned on high-level task-specific features, thereby creating a more coherent and expressive joint world model from visual input.
proposals.method: We will extend a dense prediction transformer (DPT) backbone, pre-trained via a self-supervised objective like masked image modeling, to output a shared feature pyramid. Task-specific heads will branch from this pyramid for classification, depth, and surface normal estimation. The key innovation is an inverse pathway: a causal transformer decoder (character-level) will be attached to the high-level semantic feature maps, trained via a cross-entropy loss to autoregressively generate descriptive captions (e.g., 'red car', 'steep hill') conditioned on the active task's feature channel. Training will use a multi-task loss balancing pixel-wise, classification, and textual objectives.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a unified architecture that performs joint geometric and semantic reasoning while providing interpretable textual outputs, directly linking visual features to language. It will advance multi-task learning by demonstrating that a shared representation can be explicitly decoded into descriptive text, offering a new evaluation metric for representation quality and a step towards models that explain their predictions.
proposals.novelty: While multi-task visual models exist, the integration of an inverse, task-conditioned character-level decoder to generate descriptive text from a shared visual feature pyramid is novel. This moves beyond using language as a pre-training signal (CLIP-style) to using it as a generative output modality tightly coupled with specific visual predictions.

proposals.title: Learning World Models for Graph Evolution: A Self-Supervised Framework for Anticipatory Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Real-world networks in communication, biology, and social systems evolve dynamically, driven by latent causal processes. Current graph neural networks (GNNs) primarily process static snapshots or simple temporal sequences, lacking an explicit model of the generative mechanisms behind topological changes. This limits their ability to predict future states and infer causal drivers, which is crucial for robust autonomous systems and scientific discovery.
proposals.hypothesis: By coupling a spectral GNN for node representation with a learned temporal point process or transformer-based attention mechanism that models sequences of graph events, we can infer latent drivers of evolution and learn node embeddings conditioned on anticipated future graph states, not just past observations.
proposals.method: We propose a two-component architecture: 1) A spectral GNN encoder (e.g., based on Chebyshev filters) processes the graph at each observed time step. 2) A temporal model, implemented either as a neural point process (e.g., using a Transformer Hawkes process) or a causal attention mechanism over sequences of graph events (node/edge additions/deletions), learns to predict the timing and type of future events. The system is trained via a multi-task, self-supervised objective combining next-event prediction with a contrastive loss (e.g., SimCLR-style) on node representations across time, enforcing consistency with the learned evolutionary model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a framework for learning predictive, causal models of graph dynamics, moving beyond descriptive analysis. It will deliver a new self-supervised recipe for learning representations that are robust to network evolution and capable of anticipation, directly expanding the applicability of GNNs to time-critical domains like autonomous navigation and network security.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the explicit integration of a learnable generative model for topological events with standard GNN representations, enabling the conditioning of node features on *anticipated* future graph structure, a capability absent in contemporary temporal GNNs and graph recurrent networks.

proposals.title: Learning Stable World Models on Dynamic Graphs via Arbitrary Lagrangian-Eulerian Spectral Networks
proposals.motivation: Real-world relational systems, such as social networks, sensor webs, and multi-agent interactions, are fundamentally dynamic, with nodes and edges appearing, disappearing, and evolving over time. Pre-2026 graph neural networks (GNNs) largely treat topology as static or discretely snapshotted, leading to unstable representations, temporal inconsistency, and catastrophic forgetting when applied to continuous evolution. This limits their ability to learn persistent world models for long-term reasoning in autonomous systems.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting the Arbitrary Lagrangian-Eulerian (ALE) method from Computational Fluid Dynamics to the spectral domain of graphs, we can create a neural framework that dynamically re-meshes latent representations, enabling stable, temporally consistent learning on graphs with continuously changing topology.
proposals.method: We will develop an Adaptive Spectral Network (ASN) with a core ALE-inspired operator. This operator maintains a running spectral basis (e.g., from a dynamic Laplacian) and node feature field. Upon a topological change, it performs a differentiable interpolation—akin to ALE's field remap—projecting features and the spectral basis from the old graph to the new one. This interpolated state serves as the initialization for subsequent message-passing or spectral convolutional layers, ensuring gradient flow across topology changes. The framework will be trained with a multi-task objective combining node-level prediction and a temporal consistency loss derived from the interpolation error.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, physics-inspired framework for learning on dynamic graphs, directly addressing catastrophic forgetting in non-stationary relational data. It will expand the applicability of GNNs to domains requiring long-term, stable world models, such as lifelong robotic perception and adaptive communication networks.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the direct translation of the ALE method's re-meshing and field interpolation concepts to the spectral graph learning domain, creating a continuous adaptive mechanism for graph evolution, unlike prior snapshot-based or memory-augmented approaches.

proposals.title: Joint World Modeling and Dynamic Graph Learning for Evolving Interaction Networks
proposals.motivation: Many real-world systems, such as communication networks, social networks, and biological interaction networks, are characterized by dynamic topologies where nodes and edges evolve over time. Pre-2026 graph neural networks (GNNs) largely assume static graphs, limiting their applicability to these crucial domains. This work aims to bridge the gap between self-supervised representation learning and the modeling of dynamic relational structures, a core challenge for building robust world models for autonomous systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a self-supervised learning framework, which jointly learns a predictive world model and a dynamic graph representation, can yield more robust and generalizable features for tasks in non-stationary environments than methods treating dynamics and representation learning separately.
proposals.method: We will develop a spectral-temporal GNN architecture with locally connected, adaptive filters to handle incremental node/edge changes without full retraining. The core will be a multi-task self-supervised objective combining (1) a temporal contrastive loss on node representations across graph snapshots, and (2) a masked autoencoding task for predicting future topological states, inspired by masked image modeling. This framework will be integrated with a latent variable model to learn a compact, predictive world model of the graph's evolution.
proposals.contribution: This will contribute a novel, unified framework for learning from dynamically structured data, extending the applicability of self-supervised learning beyond static images or fixed graphs. It will provide new architectural principles for incremental learning on evolving graphs, directly impacting fields like autonomous navigation in dynamic social spaces and modeling time-varying biological networks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of a predictive, latent world model—a core theme in LeCun's recent research—with the technical challenge of learning on dynamically changing graph topologies, moving beyond static graph SSL or simple temporal GNNs.

proposals.title: Joint Spectral Learning and Compression for On-Device Character-Level World Models
proposals.motivation: Deploying neural language models on microcontrollers for embodied agents requires extreme efficiency, but current compression techniques are applied post-hoc to models built on fixed, computationally expensive representations. Character-level models, which can be viewed as operating on graphs of token interactions, are promising for on-device learning but are bottlenecked by the cost of spectral graph convolutions, typically using the full Laplacian eigenbasis.
proposals.hypothesis: By co-designing the spectral basis of a graph-based character model with aggressive compression techniques, we can create ultra-efficient models where the mathematical representation itself is optimized for minimal memory and energy, enabling previously infeasible on-device learning of world models from text.
proposals.method: We will formulate character sequences as a dynamic graph and replace the fixed Laplacian eigenbasis with a learnable or selected spectral basis derived from sparsified adjacency matrices or polynomial approximations. This learned basis, optimized for sparsity and low-rank structure, will directly inform a joint training loop integrating iterative magnitude pruning and learned step-size quantization (LSQ). The final model will be a tiny, quantized graph network evaluated on next-character prediction and downstream semantic tasks for microcontrollers.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new framework for building inherently efficient character models, pushing the boundaries of on-device learning. It will provide empirical evidence that representation co-design yields superior efficiency versus separate compression, contributing a practical recipe to the self-supervised learning cookbook for embedded systems.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that compresses pre-trained models or uses fixed spectral decompositions, this proposal jointly learns the fundamental mathematical basis and the compressed model parameters, creating a synergy where the representation is born efficient.

proposals.title: Neural Functors: A Topological Field Theory Framework for Learning on General Cell Complexes
proposals.motivation: Current geometric deep learning methods (e.g., graph and manifold CNNs) are limited to specific domains like graphs or Riemannian manifolds, requiring a predefined metric or adjacency. Many real-world relational and scientific datasets, from social interactions to physical simulations, are naturally modeled as higher-order structures like simplicial complexes or hypergraphs, where traditional notions of convolution and locality are ill-defined. A principled, unified framework for signal processing on such arbitrary topological domains is a critical open problem in representation learning.
proposals.hypothesis: The axiomatic, structure-preserving approach of Topological Quantum Field Theory (TQFT) can be adapted to define a general class of neural network architectures where the fundamental learnable operations are functorially induced by the combinatorial boundary structure of the data domain, enabling effective learning without an embedded metric.
proposals.method: We will formulate a 'neural functor' that maps the category of finite cell complexes (with cobordisms as morphisms) to the category of parameterized vector spaces. Specifically, we will instantiate learnable, weight-sharing transformations on the chain complexes associated with the data domain, where the k-th boundary operator ∂_k defines the natural 'neighborhood' for signals on k-cells. This yields neural layers of the form X^(k+1) = σ( W * ∂_{k+1} X^(k) + U * ∂_{k}^T X^(k-1) ), where W, U are learnable kernels and * denotes appropriate aggregation. We will implement and test this framework on tasks involving cohomology learning, dynamical systems on simplicial complexes, and multi-relational data on hypergraphs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous mathematical framework unifying and generalizing existing geometric deep learning architectures. It will deliver practical, scalable neural network layers for data on cell complexes, enabling new applications in computational topology, physics, and network science where higher-order interactions are central.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses graph Laplacians or connection Laplacians, this approach is novel in its direct categorical formulation and its use of the entire boundary chain complex as the foundational inductive bias, moving beyond spectral methods and fixed pairwise adjacency.

proposals.title: Hierarchical, Multi-Resolution Graph World Models for Scalable Self-Supervised Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Modern Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) struggle with the joint challenges of scalability and capturing multi-scale dependencies in massive, high-dimensional graphs like social networks or 3D scenes. This limits their application in building comprehensive world models from complex relational data, a core challenge in autonomous learning systems. Drawing inspiration from multi-resolution analysis in astrophysics, which efficiently handles immense spatial datasets, offers a principled path to overcome these bottlenecks.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical GNN architecture that constructs and processes a graph at multiple, adaptively refined resolutions will learn more expressive and computationally efficient representations than flat spectral or local methods, particularly for self-supervised tasks on very large graphs.
proposals.method: We propose a two-phase framework. First, an unsupervised multi-resolution graph construction phase using iterative spectral clustering or learned graph coarsening to build a pyramid of graph representations. Second, a hierarchical message-passing network where information flows bidirectionally across scales, with gating mechanisms inspired by adaptive mesh refinement to focus computation on dynamically identified informative regions. This architecture will be trained using a multi-task self-supervised objective combining node-level reconstruction and contrastive losses across different scales of the hierarchy.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, scalable architecture for learning on massive graphs, directly extending the scalability limits of spectral and locally connected networks. It will provide a new framework for building graph-based world models where relational structure exists at multiple, interdependent levels of granularity.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the systematic translation of adaptive multi-resolution analysis from computational astrophysics into a learnable, hierarchical GNN framework, moving beyond simple graph pooling to adaptive, task-driven refinement across scales for representation learning.

proposals.title: Unified Graph World Models: Dynamic Multi-Task Loss Balancing for Scalable Representation Learning on Heterogeneous Graphs
proposals.motivation: Real-world systems like social networks and 3D environments are modeled as high-dimensional, heterogeneous graphs with multiple inherent prediction tasks (node, edge, graph-level). Pre-2026, scaling deep learning to such structures faces two core challenges: the computational complexity of processing high-dimensional relational data and the difficulty of jointly optimizing competing, imbalanced tasks on a shared representation, which hinders robust and generalizable learning.
proposals.hypothesis: A spectral graph network architecture, trained with a principled, dynamically balanced multi-task loss derived from information-theoretic and gradient conflict measures, will yield more scalable, robust, and semantically rich representations for heterogeneous graphs than single-task or naively combined multi-task approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a locally connected spectral graph neural network (GNN) backbone, inspired by convolutional inductive biases, to efficiently process high-dimensional graph structure. A key innovation is the integration of a dynamic loss-balancing module, building on pre-2026 multi-task learning theories like GradNorm or uncertainty weighting. This module will automatically adjust task weights during training based on real-time measures of task difficulty, gradient conflict, and rate of progress, ensuring stable joint optimization of node classification, link prediction, and graph regression objectives.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified, scalable framework for learning world models of complex relational systems. It will deliver a practical algorithm for dynamic multi-task loss balancing in graph domains and demonstrate its superiority on benchmarks of social networks and 3D mesh data, advancing the field of self-supervised and multi-task representation learning on structured data.
proposals.novelty: While multi-task learning and graph networks are established separately, their synergistic fusion for scalable learning on high-dimensional graphs is novel. The explicit focus on dynamic, theory-grounded loss balancing to manage inherent task conflicts on a single graph representation moves beyond simple architectural or static weighting schemes prevalent pre-2026.

proposals.title: Scaling Self-Supervised Spectral Graph Networks to High-Dimensional Data Manifolds
proposals.motivation: Current self-supervised learning frameworks for graphs, including spectral and locally connected networks, have been validated primarily on low-dimensional synthetic or citation graphs. Real-world applications like social networks, complex 3D meshes for embodied AI, and biological interaction networks present high-dimensional, non-Euclidean data manifolds where the scalability and representational power of these methods are unknown. Testing these limits is crucial for applying world model and representation learning principles to more complex, structured environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating multi-scale, spectral graph convolutional operators with a self-supervised, information-maximizing objective function, we can learn semantically rich and scalable representations from high-dimensional graph-structured data, outperforming standard geometric deep learning methods on downstream tasks.
proposals.method: We will extend the spectral framework by designing a hierarchical, locally connected network architecture that uses Chebyshev polynomial filters or adaptive graph wavelets to capture multi-scale structure. This will be trained with a joint self-supervised objective combining a contrastive task on augmented graph views (inspired by the 'Cookbook') and an information-theoretic regularization term to preserve global semantic properties. We will benchmark scalability and performance on high-dimensional datasets including large-scale social network graphs (e.g., Friendster) and complex 3D scene meshes from autonomous navigation simulators.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous evaluation of the scalability limits of modern spectral graph networks under a self-supervised paradigm. It will deliver a new, publicly available benchmark suite for high-dimensional graph representation learning and a trained model that can serve as a foundational world model component for complex, structured environments in vision and robotics.
proposals.novelty: The integration of multi-scale spectral filters with a multi-task, information-maximizing self-supervised objective specifically tailored for the high-dimensional regime is unexplored. Moving beyond low-dimensional benchmarks to test these methods on data from embodied AI and large social networks represents a significant step in complexity.

proposals.title: Learning to Route: Adaptive Spectral-Local Gating for Robust Graph Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) face a fundamental trade-off: spectral methods excel at capturing global structure but can oversmooth, while local message-passing is flexible but can be myopic. Pre-2026 work in self-supervised learning, including LeCun's world model research, highlights the need for models that learn robust, multi-scale representations without fixed inductive biases. A single, static architecture cannot optimally process the heterogeneous regions of complex real-world graphs, such as those in scene understanding or social networks.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid GNN with a learned, adaptive gating mechanism that dynamically routes node representations between spectral and local processing pathways will yield more robust and efficient graph representations than static architectures, by applying the most appropriate inductive bias to each part of the graph.
proposals.method: We propose the Adaptively Gated Hybrid Network (AGHN). Each layer contains a Spectral Filter Bank (Chebyshev polynomials for efficiency) and a Local Message-Passing operator (e.g., GAT). The core is the Adaptive Spectral-Local Router, a lightweight MLP that takes the node's current representation and local subgraph statistics as input and outputs a soft routing weight. This weight gates the outputs of the two parallel processing streams, which are then combined. The entire system, including the router, is trained end-to-end via a joint objective combining task-specific loss (e.g., node classification) and an auxiliary self-supervised reconstruction loss to encourage meaningful routing decisions.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new, dynamically routed hybrid architecture for graphs, moving beyond simple ensemble or sequential designs. It will provide a framework for learning task-adaptive inductive biases and yield empirical gains in robustness and efficiency on benchmark tasks. The analysis of the learned routing patterns will offer insights into when spectral versus local processing is most beneficial.
proposals.novelty: While hybrid GNNs exist, the key novelty is the conditional, per-node, per-layer routing mechanism. This allows the model to learn a dynamic, context-dependent processing strategy, a concept aligned with but distinct from the mixture-of-experts and modular reasoning themes in pre-2026 AI, now applied to the spectral-vs-spatial dichotomy in graph learning.

proposals.title: A Renormalization Group Framework for Stability and Universality in Graph Convolutional Networks
proposals.motivation: Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNNs) lack a rigorous theoretical framework for understanding their stability to graph perturbations and their generalization across different graph topologies. This limits their reliable application in critical domains like molecular property prediction and social network analysis. Insights from statistical physics, particularly the Renormalization Group (RG), which explains universality and scale-invariant behavior, offer a powerful but underexplored analogy for analyzing multi-scale representation learning in deep networks.
proposals.hypothesis: The learning dynamics and final representations of a GCNN can be modeled as a flow in parameter space under a formally defined graph coarse-graining operation, analogous to an RG flow. The fixed points and relevant/irrelevant operators of this flow will determine the model's stability to perturbations and define universality classes for generalization across graph structures.
proposals.method: We will define a graph renormalization procedure using successive spectral graph coarsening or differentiable pooling layers (e.g., DiffPool) to create a hierarchy of scaled-down graphs. We will then derive the corresponding flow equations for the GCNN's parameters (e.g., filter weights) as we move through this hierarchy, inspired by RG techniques. This theoretical framework will be validated by analyzing the stability of trained models to edge deletions/additions (graph perturbations) and by measuring the similarity of parameter flows for graphs from different generative models (e.g., Erdős–Rényi, Barabási–Albert).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new theoretical lens for analyzing GCNNs, delivering formal, physics-inspired definitions of stability and generalization. It will yield practical criteria for predicting model performance on unseen graph topologies and a principled comparison of the multi-scale representation capabilities of GCNNs versus standard CNNs on grid-like data.
proposals.novelty: While RG ideas have been loosely mentioned in deep learning (e.g., for understanding hierarchical features), this proposal provides the first rigorous adaptation of the full RG formalism—with its flow equations and operator analysis—specifically to the domain of graph-structured data and GCNNs.

proposals.title: Stable and Adaptive Multi-Task Graph Neural Networks: A Theoretical Framework for Generalization and Loss Balancing
proposals.motivation: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are widely applied to multi-task problems on relational data, such as joint node classification and link prediction. However, existing multi-task GNNs lack a theoretical foundation for their stability to graph perturbations and their generalization across different graph topologies. Furthermore, they rely on heuristic methods for balancing multiple loss functions, which can be suboptimal and unstable, hindering robust performance in real-world applications like autonomous navigation and semantic scene understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by synthesizing tools from graph signal processing and multi-task learning theory, we can derive explicit stability and generalization bounds for multi-task GNNs. Furthermore, we posit that an adaptive loss-balancing mechanism, explicitly designed to optimize these bounds, will yield more stable and generalizable models than existing heuristic approaches.
proposals.method: We will first extend spectral graph convolution stability analysis to the multi-task setting, deriving Rademacher complexity-based generalization bounds that account for task relationships. Building on this, we will formulate the multi-task GNN training as a bilevel optimization problem: the inner loop trains the network parameters, while the outer loop adapts the task loss weights using a meta-gradient derived from the theoretical stability and generalization bounds. This will be implemented within a GNN architecture supporting both node- and graph-level tasks, with validation on datasets featuring structural noise and multiple prediction targets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first unified theoretical framework for stability and generalization in multi-task GNNs. It will yield a principled, theory-driven algorithm for adaptive loss balancing that directly optimizes for robustness, moving beyond empirical heuristics like uncertainty weighting. The resulting closed-loop system will offer practitioners a theoretically grounded tool for building more reliable multi-task models on graph-structured data.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the formal fusion of GNN stability theory with multi-task learning generalization bounds to create a new adaptive loss-balancing paradigm. Unlike prior work, the balancing mechanism is not heuristic but is explicitly derived to optimize the derived theoretical guarantees, creating a direct theory-to-practice feedback loop.

proposals.title: Learning Multi-Scale World Models with Graph Wavelet Pyramids
proposals.motivation: A core challenge in self-supervised learning for complex, non-grid data (e.g., scene graphs, knowledge graphs, or physical interaction graphs) is building multi-scale representations that capture both local structure and long-range dependencies. Spectral Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNNs) are limited by filters with global support, which is computationally inefficient and lacks the spatial locality crucial for hierarchical reasoning and efficient world model learning.
proposals.hypothesis: By constructing a graph wavelet pyramid through recursive coarsening, we can define localized, multi-scale filters that enable efficient and expressive graph convolutions, directly analogous to multi-resolution analysis in computer vision, and this will significantly improve the learning of hierarchical world models from graph-structured data.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Laplacian pyramid construction from GIS/vision to graphs. Using the Graclus algorithm for recursive graph coarsening, we will build a hierarchy of increasingly coarse graphs. At each level, we will define graph wavelet filters as the difference between signal representations on a graph and its upsampled, coarser counterpart, generating localized detail coefficients. These wavelet filters will serve as the convolutional kernels in a novel GCNN architecture, enabling message passing within and across scales for multi-scale representation learning.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, locally-supported alternative to spectral graph filters, enabling scalable and hierarchical processing of large graphs. It directly contributes to the 'cookbook' of self-supervised learning by offering a new method for learning multi-scale world models from relational and spatial data, with applications in autonomous navigation and semantic scene understanding.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the direct translation of the established, grid-based wavelet pyramid—a cornerstone of multi-scale image analysis—into the domain of graph neural networks via a graph coarsening hierarchy, creating truly localized spectral-like filters, which has not been fully realized in pre-2026 GCNN literature.

proposals.title: Jointly Learned Hierarchical Coarsening for Task-Driven Graph Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Hierarchical graph pooling is crucial for scaling Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to large graphs and capturing multi-scale structural semantics, a core challenge in representation learning. However, pre-2026 methods often rely on fixed, task-agnostic coarsening (e.g., using spectral clustering or node dropping), which may not align with the downstream objective, limiting the quality of the learned representations for tasks like classification or regression. This disconnect between the structural abstraction and the end-task is a significant bottleneck in building effective and generalizable graph world models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a differentiable, lightweight module that learns to coarsen a graph by producing soft node assignments, optimized end-to-end for a specific task, will discover more semantically meaningful hierarchies than predefined methods, leading to superior graph representations and task performance.
proposals.method: We propose a novel graph pooling layer where a small neural network, conditioned on the current node features and adjacency matrix, outputs a soft assignment matrix. This matrix defines a probabilistic pooling of nodes into super-nodes for the next hierarchical level. The module's parameters are learned via standard backpropagation through the entire GNN, directly minimizing the final task loss (e.g., cross-entropy). We will explore architectural variants, including attention mechanisms for assignment and constraints to ensure sparsity and connectivity preservation, building on concepts from differentiable clustering and recent self-supervised learning cookbooks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, fully differentiable building block for hierarchical GNNs that adapts pooling to the task. It will provide empirical evidence on benchmark datasets that task-adaptive coarsening outperforms static methods in graph classification and regression. The resulting framework advances the goal of learning world models for complex relational data by making structural abstraction a learned, integral part of the representation learning process.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that separates graph coarsening from network training, our method makes the hierarchical structure itself a learnable function of the data and the task objective, enabling the discovery of novel, task-specific graph abstractions through gradient-based optimization.

proposals.title: Learning Hierarchical World Models via Renormalization Group-Inspired Graph Pooling
proposals.motivation: Current graph pooling methods, such as DiffPool or SAGPool, often rely on heuristic clustering or node selection, lacking a principled framework for preserving the essential relational and semantic structure of the data across scales. This limits the ability of graph neural networks to learn multi-scale world models, a core challenge in autonomous navigation and visual representation learning. A more systematic, learnable coarse-graining procedure is needed to build hierarchical representations that are both efficient and semantically meaningful.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a graph pooling operator, explicitly designed to mimic the Renormalization Group (RG) flow from statistical physics, will produce a hierarchy of coarsened graphs where each level preserves the critical relational invariants of the original data, leading to superior performance in downstream tasks requiring multi-scale reasoning.
proposals.method: We will design a parametric RG-Pool layer. For a given graph, it will learn a soft assignment matrix to group nodes into super-nodes (blocks) by minimizing a loss that combines a reconstruction term for node features and a constraint to preserve spectral properties of the graph Laplacian. The effective feature for a super-node will be a learned, weighted aggregation of its constituent nodes' features. The new adjacency matrix will be computed as a function of the original adjacency and the assignment matrix, analogous to deriving an effective Hamiltonian. This layer will be stacked within a GNN encoder and trained end-to-end using a multi-task objective combining self-supervised graph-level prediction and supervised node/ graph classification.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, physics-inspired pooling mechanism that provides a more interpretable and structured hierarchy for graph representations. It will advance the field of self-supervised learning on graphs by enabling the construction of better world models from relational data.
