The method will be validated on complex, multi-scale tasks such as scene graph understanding from images and long-horizon prediction for navigation.
proposals.novelty: While RG analogies have been suggested in deep learning (e.g., in deep belief networks), applying the formal coarse-graining and effective theory framework directly to learnable graph pooling is novel. This moves beyond simple topological clustering to a data-driven preservation of a graph's 'critical' relational semantics across scales.

proposals.title: Inverting Graph Pooling Hierarchies for Controlled, Semantic Image Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Current image generation models, like diffusion models, lack fine-grained, structured control over scene composition and object relationships, making precise semantic manipulation difficult. Conversely, discriminative models for detection or segmentation learn rich hierarchical representations of scene structure. Bridging these paradigms—using learned structural graphs as a generative blueprint—is a critical step towards more controllable and semantically grounded image synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: The graph coarsening hierarchy learned by a discriminative object detection network, which captures multi-scale semantic communities, can be inverted to provide a structural scaffold for a generative model, enabling precise, context-aware manipulation of image content.
proposals.method: We will first train a graph-based object detector (e.g., based on a Transformer or GNN backbone) to produce a hierarchy of coarsened graph representations via learned pooling. This hierarchy will be frozen. We then train a conditional generative model (e.g., a latent diffusion model) where the denoising process is conditioned not just on text, but on features from a specific node in this frozen graph hierarchy. A cross-attention mechanism will fuse the graph's semantic and relational features with the generative process, and a dedicated graph upsampling decoder will invert the pooling steps to map coarse graph edits back to pixel-space modifications.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new method for semantically controlled image generation and editing by directly leveraging the structural priors of discriminative models. It will demonstrate a novel fusion of graph-based scene understanding with generative models, enabling tasks like object insertion with respect to scale and occlusion. The resulting framework will provide a testbed for studying the invertibility of learned visual representations.
proposals.novelty: While graph neural networks are used in both generation and detection, the explicit inversion of a *discriminatively learned* graph pooling hierarchy to guide a generative process is novel. This approach treats the detection graph not just as a feature but as a reversible, semantic program for image synthesis.

proposals.title: Learning Hierarchical Representations via Graph Coarsening for Self-Supervised World Models
proposals.motivation: In self-supervised learning for vision, defining effective pooling hierarchies for locally connected networks (e.g., convolutional or graph neural networks) remains a challenge. Standard pooling operations like max-pooling are heuristic and may discard semantically important relational structures. Transferring principled graph coarsening algorithms from network science could provide a more data-driven and semantically meaningful way to build multi-scale representations, which is critical for learning world models from visual data.
proposals.hypothesis: That applying advanced graph coarsening and community detection algorithms (e.g., Louvain, Graclus, or spectral coarsening) to define pooling hierarchies in a neural network will yield more efficient and semantically structured representations than fixed geometric pooling, leading to improved sample efficiency and generalization in self-supervised learning tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework where the feature maps of a convolutional network are treated as signals on a locally connected graph. At each layer, we will compute a coarsened graph using algorithms like the Louvain method for community detection or spectral coarsening, which groups nodes based on feature similarity and connectivity. These learned groupings will define the pooling regions, downsizing the graph signal while preserving community structure. This learnable coarsening module will be integrated into a self-supervised learning pipeline, such as a joint-embedding architecture, and trained end-to-end on large-scale image or video datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel, principled method for adaptive, structure-aware pooling in deep learning. It will provide a bridge between network science and representation learning, demonstrating how graph-theoretic algorithms can optimize neural network architecture. The resulting models are expected to show improved efficiency in downstream tasks like autonomous navigation and semantic understanding by learning more meaningful multi-scale abstractions.
proposals.novelty: While graph pooling exists in GNN literature, applying advanced, general-purpose graph coarsening algorithms to define the pooling hierarchy in standard convolutional architectures for self-supervised visual representation learning is unexplored. This moves beyond fixed geometric patterns to learn pooling structures directly from data correlations.

proposals.title: Spectral Graph Monte Carlo: Scalable, Matrix-Free Learning with Stochastic Diffusion
proposals.motivation: Spectral graph neural networks (GNNs) and signal processing techniques are powerful for learning on non-Euclidean data but are fundamentally limited by the need for eigen-decomposition or polynomial approximations of the graph Laplacian, which does not scale to web-scale graphs with billions of nodes. Current approximation methods like Chebyshev polynomials or Lanczos still require matrix-vector multiplications that become prohibitive in memory and computation for such massive, dynamically evolving graphs common in real-world learning tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: The core stochastic sampling strategies of Quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) methods can be adapted to create a scalable, unbiased estimator for spectral graph filters, enabling efficient, matrix-free computation of localized spectral features through guided random walks on the graph structure.
proposals.method: We will develop a Spectral Graph Monte Carlo (SGMC) framework where a target spectral filter function (e.g., a low-pass or band-pass filter) is approximated by the expected outcome of a stochastic process. Specifically, we will design a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) procedure on the graph, where random walks or diffusion processes are generated with transition probabilities derived from the graph Laplacian. The walks will be weighted and terminated probabilistically according to a scheme inspired by QMC's 'importance sampling' and 'reptation' concepts, such that the ensemble average of a function of the walk statistics yields an unbiased estimate of the filtered signal. This will be integrated into a self-supervised learning pipeline to train graph representations by defining pretext tasks based on stochastic spectral feature reconstruction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a fundamentally new algorithmic primitive for scalable graph learning, enabling the application of spectral methods to previously intractable graph sizes. It will contribute a theoretical bridge between stochastic approximation in computational physics and machine learning, and deliver a practical, open-source implementation for large-scale, self-supervised representation learning on graph-structured data like social networks or knowledge graphs.
proposals.novelty: While stochastic methods exist for graph problems, the direct analogy to Quantum Monte Carlo's strategy for estimating quantum observables to approximate spectral filters is novel. This approach moves beyond deterministic polynomial approximations, offering a probabilistic, inherently parallelizable, and memory-efficient alternative without explicit Laplacian matrix operations.

proposals.title: Efficient Spectral World Models for Long-Range Sequence Understanding
proposals.motivation: Current models for long sequences, like Transformers, scale quadratically with sequence length, making book-length or high-resolution multimodal data processing inefficient. Convolutional alternatives, while efficient, struggle with truly long-range dependencies. This creates a bottleneck for building world models that understand long-context narratives or physical processes from raw sequential data, a core challenge in autonomous learning systems.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling long sequences as implicit graphs and applying scalable spectral approximation techniques, we can construct globally-informed convolutional filters that capture long-range dependencies with sub-quadratic complexity, enabling more efficient and effective sequence modeling for large-alphabet data like Unicode text or symbolic sensory streams.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel neural architecture where input sequences define implicit graph structures over tokens. We will approximate spectral graph filters on these structures using Chebyshev polynomials or the randomized Nyström method to avoid full eigen-decomposition. These approximated filters will be integrated into a convolutional network backbone, potentially within a multi-scale framework, and trained with a joint objective combining masked token prediction (self-supervised learning) and a downstream semantic task to learn transferable representations.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, computationally efficient architecture for long-sequence modeling that bridges spectral graph theory and deep learning. It will provide a practical alternative to attention for processing book-length documents or complex symbolic data. The outcomes will advance scalable self-supervised learning by enabling world models to integrate information over much longer temporal horizons.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of scalable spectral approximations with implicit graph construction for sequence data is unexplored, moving beyond their traditional use in static graphs. This approach offers a fundamentally different inductive bias for capturing global context compared to localized convolutions or attention mechanisms.

proposals.title: Scalable Spectral World Models: Efficient Graph Representation Learning for Large-Scale Visual and Semantic Tasks
proposals.motivation: Spectral graph neural networks offer powerful inductive biases for learning structured world models from relational data, such as scene graphs or semantic knowledge graphs derived from visual inputs. However, their reliance on full eigen-decomposition of the graph Laplacian makes them computationally intractable for the large, dynamic graphs encountered in real-world autonomous systems and large-scale self-supervised learning. Pre-2026, scalable approximations exist but have not been deeply integrated into end-to-end learning frameworks for complex, multi-task representation learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating scalable spectral approximation techniques—specifically, Chebyshev polynomial filters and randomized Nyström approximations—into a joint training framework, we can learn rich, multi-scale world model representations from massive graphs without explicit eigen-decomposition, enabling efficient learning on tasks requiring relational reasoning over large state spaces.
proposals.method: We will develop a spectral network architecture where localized spectral filters are approximated via truncated Chebyshev polynomials for fast convolution, and global graph coarsening uses a Nyström-based low-rank approximation of the graph Laplacian for scalable pooling. This will be integrated into a self-supervised learning pipeline, inspired by the 'cookbook' of SSL, where the model is trained to predict masked nodes and edges in large semantic graphs constructed from multi-view image datasets. We will leverage incremental learning strategies to handle dynamically evolving graph structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical, scalable framework for spectral graph representation learning, directly applicable to large-scale visual and semantic tasks in autonomous navigation and world modeling. It will provide empirical benchmarks on massive graph datasets (e.g., ImageNet-derived scene graphs) and release open-source modules to bridge the gap between theoretical spectral methods and applied deep learning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the co-design of specific approximation algorithms (Chebyshev/Nyström) with a multi-task, self-supervised objective for world model learning, moving beyond proof-of-concept scalability to demonstrate superior efficiency and representation quality on concrete, large-scale vision and navigation problems.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Graph Partitioning for Extreme-Scale Self-Supervised Learning on Web-Scale Graphs
proposals.motivation: Training neural networks directly on massive, sparse graphs like the entire web graph is computationally prohibitive due to the O(N²) scaling of interactions between nodes. Current graph neural networks and self-supervised learning methods struggle with this extreme scale and irregular connectivity. Drawing inspiration from computational cosmology, which efficiently simulates N-body gravitational interactions using hierarchical tree algorithms, offers a principled path to overcome this bottleneck.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting the Barnes-Hut algorithm and domain decomposition techniques from cosmological N-body simulations to the structure of massive graphs, we can develop a hierarchical, approximate graph partitioning and computation scheme that reduces the complexity of training locally connected networks on web-scale graphs from O(N²) to O(N log N).
proposals.method: We will develop a framework that constructs a hierarchical tree (e.g., an octree or k-d tree) over the graph's embedding space or connectivity structure. Distant clusters of nodes in this hierarchy will be aggregated into single 'super-nodes' during forward/backward passes, analogous to treating distant galaxy clusters as single masses. This requires new sparse tensor operations and a distributed training scheme where gradient updates for aggregated clusters are approximated. We will test this on self-supervised learning tasks, such as link prediction or node representation learning, using multi-view contrastive objectives on subgraphs of the web.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational framework for training neural networks on previously intractable, planet-scale graphs. It will advance the field of self-supervised representation learning by enabling the direct use of the web's full graph structure. The resulting hierarchical partitioning algorithms and sparse operators will be a contribution to large-scale machine learning systems.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the direct translation of N-body simulation algorithms (Barnes-Hut) to the problem of graph-based neural network training, a cross-disciplinary analogy not previously explored. This moves beyond standard graph partitioning or sampling methods by introducing a physics-inspired, multi-resolution approximation scheme for activations and gradients.

proposals.title: JEPA-Graph: Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures on Adaptive Visual Graphs for Holistic Scene Understanding
proposals.motivation: Current vision architectures, including standard ConvNets and Vision Transformers, rely on fixed or uniform receptive fields and often process images via sliding windows or patches, which is computationally inefficient for high-resolution, holistic scene understanding. This precludes a truly global, data-adaptive analysis where object detection and segmentation emerge from a single, coherent representation of the entire visual field, a key step towards more autonomous, efficient perception systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by modeling an image as a sparse graph where nodes are visual primitives (e.g., superpixels) and using a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) to learn adaptive, multi-scale receptive fields through graph neural networks, we can achieve superior efficiency and accuracy in holistic scene parsing from global context compared to exhaustive multi-scale scanning methods.
proposals.method: We will construct an irregular graph from an image using superpixel segmentation or learned visual tokens. A multi-layer, sparse Graph Neural Network (GNN) will propagate information across this graph, with edge structures dynamically pruned or reinforced based on learned semantic similarity. This core will be integrated into a JEPA framework, where the predictor network learns to infer latent representations of distant graph regions, implicitly forming adaptive, hierarchical receptive fields. Training will leverage a combination of self-supervised objectives (e.g., masked region prediction) and downstream supervised tasks for object detection and segmentation on datasets like COCO and Cityscapes.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, scalable architecture for end-to-end global scene understanding that eliminates the need for hand-crafted multi-scale pyramids. It will demonstrate how JEPA principles can be applied to irregular graph structures to learn task-adaptive receptive fields, pushing the frontier of efficient, high-resolution visual perception.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of a JEPA-based predictive world model with a dynamically structured visual graph, enabling receptive fields to form based on semantic content rather than fixed spatial priors, moving beyond the patch-based or sliding-window paradigms dominant in pre-2026 vision models.

proposals.title: World-Scale Graph Learning: Scalable Self-Supervised Training of Locally Connected Networks on Trillion-Edge Graphs
proposals.motivation: The web, social networks, and knowledge graphs represent massive, sparse relational datasets where global connectivity patterns encode rich semantic information. Current graph neural networks (GNNs) struggle with the extreme scale and irregular structure of these real-world graphs, limiting our ability to learn universal representations from such foundational data sources. This project aims to overcome these scalability barriers, enabling the application of self-supervised learning principles, championed by LeCun, to world-scale graph-structured data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by developing a sparse, distributed training framework specifically optimized for the power-law and hierarchical connectivity of real-world graphs, we can train extreme-scale locally connected networks that learn transferable, multi-scale semantic representations in a self-supervised manner.
proposals.method: We will design a novel, hierarchical graph partitioning scheme that minimizes communication overhead for distributed sparse tensor operations, preserving local neighborhood structure. We will implement a self-supervised training objective based on latent-variable energy-based models (as in LeCun's world model research), using multi-task learning over local graph diffusion processes (e.g., predicting masked subgraphs or node/edge attributes). The core architecture will be a massively parallel, sparse variant of a Graph Convolutional Network, optimized using incremental learning techniques to handle dynamic graph updates.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a new, open-source distributed training system for trillion-edge graphs, 2) a set of self-supervised tasks proven to learn robust graph representations, and 3) empirical results demonstrating the transferability of learned representations to downstream tasks like node classification and link prediction at an unprecedented scale.
proposals.novelty: While distributed GNN training exists, our approach uniquely combines LeCun's self-supervised learning 'cookbook' with a system-level focus on the specific irregular sparsity patterns of real-world web-scale graphs, moving beyond small-scale or synthetically partitioned benchmarks.

proposals.title: Active Bayesian Graph Discovery via Strategic Probing of Graph Convolutional Networks
proposals.motivation: Inferring graph structure from observed data is a fundamental but ill-posed inverse problem in relational learning. Current methods for graph inference from Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNNs) are passive, relying on static input-output pairs, which leads to ambiguity and poor identifiability. An active learning framework that strategically queries a trained model is needed to efficiently disambiguate the underlying topology, directly addressing a core challenge in representation learning for relational data.
proposals.hypothesis: That a closed-loop, active probing strategy, guided by an information-theoretic criterion and constrained by a Bayesian posterior over graphs, can efficiently and uniquely identify the graph structure encoded within a trained GCNN, overcoming the inherent ill-posedness of passive inference.
proposals.method: We will develop a system that maintains a Bayesian posterior over graph structures, incorporating spectral constraints from the known GCNN filters (e.g., Chebyshev or polynomial) and sparsity-promoting structural priors. An acquisition function based on expected information gain (e.g., mutual information) will select optimal probe signals (node feature vectors) to submit to the GCNN. The resulting node or graph-level activations will be used to update the posterior via likelihood models derived from the GCNN's forward pass, iteratively refining the graph estimate.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, sample-efficient framework for the inverse problem of graph discovery from trained models. It will advance the field of self-supervised and multi-task learning by enabling the extraction of interpretable relational structures from learned representations. The methodology bridges active learning, Bayesian inference, and geometric deep learning in a novel way.
proposals.novelty: The integration of active, strategic probing with Bayesian graph inference for GCNNs is novel, moving beyond passive observational methods. Combining spectral constraints from the network architecture with information-theoretic optimal experimental design for graph discovery is a distinct, non-obvious synthesis of pre-2026 concepts.

proposals.title: Inverse Graph Structure Discovery via Spectral Phase Retrieval in Pre-Trained Graph Convolutional Networks
proposals.motivation: A core challenge in graph machine learning is the frequent absence of explicit, reliable graph structure, which is essential for Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNNs). Current methods for graph inference often rely on heuristics or are entangled with representation learning, limiting their applicability to pre-trained models. This work addresses the inverse problem of recovering an unknown graph topology solely from observed node signals and the known parameters of a pre-trained GCNN, enabling structure discovery in domains where relational data is implicit or corrupted.
proposals.hypothesis: The forward pass of a fixed, pre-trained GCNN acts as a scattering operation analogous to X-ray diffraction, such that the graph's adjacency matrix can be uniquely reconstructed from its output activations by solving a constrained spectral phase retrieval problem.
proposals.method: We will formalize the GCNN's spectral filtering operation as a transformation where the measurable 'diffraction pattern' is the set of node representations. The core algorithm will adapt iterative projection methods from crystallography, such as the Hybrid Input-Output (HIO) algorithm. These algorithms will be constrained by the known spectral response of the GCNN's filters (acting as the known 'lens') and by graph-specific priors like sparsity, connectivity, and non-negativity of the adjacency matrix. The reconstruction pipeline will involve an alternating optimization between the Fourier (spectral) domain, defined by the GCNN's graph Laplacian eigenvectors, and the primal domain of the adjacency matrix.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a novel, principled framework for graph topology inference that decouples structure discovery from representation learning, allowing the 'inversion' of pre-trained models. It will advance the field of self-supervised learning on graphs by offering a new method to learn world models—implicit graph structures—from observational data and fixed feature extractors, a step towards more autonomous and interpretable systems.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the direct analogy to crystallography's phase problem, translating its well-established reconstruction algorithms to the domain of graph inference. This approach is distinct from contemporary graph learning or generative methods, as it treats a trained GCNN as a fixed, known physical system whose internal 'scattering' must be inverted.

proposals.title: Self-Supervised Graph Inference for Adaptive, Efficient On-Device Vision
proposals.motivation: Deploying adaptive, context-aware vision models on resource-constrained devices remains a key challenge. While graph neural networks and attention mechanisms can model relational structure, their computational cost is prohibitive for mobile use, and static architectures cannot dynamically adjust to input content. This work addresses the need for vision systems that can discover and leverage input-specific structure in real-time to optimize their own inference.
proposals.hypothesis: A lightweight, quantized convolutional network can perform a single forward pass that simultaneously extracts features and infers a sparse, task-relevant graph over those features, and that this inferred graph can be used to dynamically guide and sparsify subsequent computation, leading to greater efficiency without sacrificing accuracy.
proposals.method: We will design a quantized, mobile-optimized network inspired by OverFeat's multi-scale efficiency. A small, parallel 'inverse' head will process intermediate feature maps using a differentiable, sparsity-promoting process (e.g., a learned, lightweight variant of probabilistic graphical model inference or sparse attention) to output a sparse adjacency matrix. This graph, representing semantic or spatial relationships, will then gate message-passing operations in later network stages or in a subsequent lightweight GNN module, effectively pruning unnecessary computations. The entire system will be trained end-to-end using a self-supervised objective from a 'cookbook' of pretext tasks, such as jigsaw puzzle solving or contrastive predictive coding, to learn generally useful representations and graph inference.
proposals.contribution: This research will contribute a novel architecture for efficient, adaptive on-device vision that fuses representation learning with dynamic graph inference. It will provide a concrete framework for input-dependent computational sparsification and advance methods for learning world models—understood as latent graph structures—directly from sensory data. The resulting models will be validated on embedded navigation and multi-object scene understanding tasks.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the tight, efficient fusion of feature extraction and online graph structure discovery within a single forward pass of a mobile network, enabling immediate feedback for adaptive computation. This moves beyond static graph inference or post-hoc analysis to create a self-optimizing visual system.

proposals.title: Inverse Graph Inference: Recovering Topology from Signals and a Trained Graph Convolutional Network
proposals.motivation: Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs) have become a standard tool for learning on structured data, but they typically assume the graph topology is known. In many real-world scenarios, such as in sensor networks or biological systems, we observe node signals but the underlying relational structure is hidden or noisy. Solving the inverse problem of inferring the graph from data and a trained model would enable structure discovery in complex systems, a core challenge in unsupervised representation learning. This aligns with the drive to move beyond supervised learning on fixed graphs and towards models that can reason about the structure of the world.
proposals.hypothesis: The learned filters and node representations (activations) of a GCN trained on observed signals contain sufficient information to recover the original or an equivalent graph topology through an optimization process. The graph can be inferred by enforcing consistency between the model's forward propagation and the observed signal statistics.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage, self-supervised approach. First, we train a standard GCN on the observed node signals using a pretext task, such as signal reconstruction or contrastive learning, to obtain meaningful node embeddings and filter parameters. Second, we fix the trained GCN parameters and formulate the adjacency matrix as a learnable, differentiable variable. We then optimize this graph topology by minimizing a loss that combines a reconstruction term (signals generated through the fixed GCN should match the input statistics) with sparsity and smoothness priors. The optimization will use gradient-based methods, treating the adjacency matrix as a continuous object with a Gumbel-Softmax or iterative thresholding step to enforce discreteness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for joint representation and structure learning, extending the utility of GCNs to domains with unknown relational structure. It contributes a new inverse problem formulation to the geometric deep learning community. The method could serve as a tool for scientific discovery, inferring interaction networks from observational data where explicit graph data is unavailable.
proposals.novelty: While graph learning from signals exists, the novel angle is leveraging the internal parameters and propagation rules of a *pre-trained* GCN as a strong prior and constraint for topology inference. This inverts the standard GCN workflow, using the model not just for prediction but as a guide for structure discovery, connecting model activations directly to underlying geometry.

proposals.title: JEPA-Inspired Continual Learning on Graphs: A Self-Supervised Framework for Dynamic Embedded Environments
proposals.motivation: Deploying graph neural networks (GNNs) on embedded IoT and sensor devices is challenging due to dynamic graph structures and non-stationary data streams. Static, pre-trained models fail over time, while full retraining is computationally prohibitive. This work addresses the critical need for robust, lightweight systems that can adapt continually under severe memory and compute constraints.
proposals.hypothesis: A system combining a frozen, quantized base GNN for efficient inference with a small, event-triggered adaptive module trained via self-supervised objectives can achieve continual adaptation to dynamic graphs on-device, mitigating catastrophic forgetting without requiring external data storage.
proposals.method: We propose a two-component architecture: 1) A heavily quantized, static GNN encoder for feature extraction, and 2) a small, trainable 'prediction head' implementing a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) for graphs. The JEPA head is trained continually via a self-supervised task (e.g., predicting masked node features or latent subgraph states) on streaming data. We employ dynamic sparse training to keep the active parameter count low and a constrained experience replay buffer using coresets or generative replay, all within fixed device memory. Adaptation is triggered by novelty detection metrics on incoming graph data.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel framework for continual, self-supervised learning on graphs in resource-constrained settings. It will advance the field of embedded machine learning by providing a principled method for lifelong adaptation, moving beyond static model compression. The resulting system will be empirically validated on dynamic sensor network and IoT datasets.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the fusion of LeCun's JEPA-based self-supervised learning principles with the constraints of continual learning on embedded devices, applied specifically to dynamic graph data. This moves beyond simple fine-tuning or static quantization by enabling structured, predictive world model updates directly on the edge.

proposals.title: Event-Driven Graph Neural Networks: Self-Supervised Learning of Sparse, Adaptive Representations for Edge Computing
proposals.motivation: Processing dynamic graph-structured data (e.g., from IoT sensor networks) on resource-constrained edge devices remains a critical challenge. Traditional Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) perform dense, synchronous message passing, which is computationally and energetically prohibitive for battery-powered deployment. Neuromorphic computing principles offer a promising but underexplored path toward extreme efficiency through event-driven, sparse communication.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating event-driven, spike-based communication principles from neuromorphic systems into GNNs, we can learn adaptive, sparse graph representations that maintain task performance while drastically reducing computation and communication overhead, enabling sustainable on-device learning.
proposals.method: We will design an Edge-Adaptive Graph Network (EAGN) where nodes are spiking neural units. Message passing is conditional: a node emits a spike (and transmits its state) only when a learned, internal membrane potential exceeds a threshold. We will develop a self-supervised training framework, inspired by world model and contrastive learning principles, to learn these thresholds and spike encodings directly from multi-modal sensor data (e.g., image and semantic streams). The training will leverage surrogate gradient methods for backpropagation through the sparse, asynchronous system.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel class of event-driven GNNs that are inherently efficient for embedded deployment. It will provide a principled framework for applying neuromorphic computing principles to graph-structured data learning, advancing the field of energy-aware machine learning. The resulting models will be validated on real-world edge navigation and sensor network tasks.
proposals.novelty: While event-based vision and spiking neural networks exist, their principled integration into adaptive graph learning for general sensor networks is novel. This moves beyond applying fixed sparsity or quantization by making sparsity a dynamic, learned property of the representation itself.

proposals.title: Multi-Task-Aware Differentiable Architecture Search for Constrained Graph Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Deploying graph neural networks (GNNs) on resource-constrained IoT devices requires ultra-lightweight architectures, but current Neural Architecture Search (NAS) methods optimize primarily for single-task efficiency, ignoring the multi-task nature of real-world sensor networks. Naively summing losses for tasks like node classification and anomaly detection leads to suboptimal shared representations under severe parametric constraints, a critical gap for embedded intelligence. This work addresses the need for hardware-efficient models that are inherently structured for robust multi-task learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that directly integrating a theoretically-grounded, adaptive multi-task loss-balancing mechanism into the differentiable NAS search loop will yield graph network architectures that achieve superior Pareto-optimal trade-offs between model size, inference cost, and multi-task performance compared to sequential NAS-then-MTL approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a differentiable NAS framework where the search space consists of quantizable and prunable GNN operations (e.g., lightweight message-passing variants). The core innovation is a multi-task-aware search objective using a gradient-based loss-balancing algorithm (inspired by recent MTL theory) that dynamically weights task losses during architecture parameter optimization. The search will be constrained by hardware-cost proxies (FLOPs, memory) for target microcontrollers, and the final architecture will be trained via our cookbook of self-supervised and supervised techniques to learn optimal shared representations.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel NAS framework that co-discovers hardware-efficient and multi-task-optimal graph architectures, 2) a set of ultra-lightweight GNNs for embedded multi-task inference, validated on IoT-relevant graph datasets, and 3) new insights into the interaction between loss landscapes, architecture selection, and representation sharing under extreme compression.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight, synergistic integration of adaptive multi-task learning theory directly into the architecture search process for graph networks, moving beyond treating NAS and MTL as separate stages. This is distinct from prior work that applies NAS to find a single efficient backbone before multi-task fine-tuning.

proposals.title: Self-Supervised World Models for Lightweight, On-Device Graph Neural Networks
proposals.motivation: The deployment of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) on resource-constrained IoT and edge devices is hindered by their computational and memory footprint. Pre-2026, efficient GNNs often rely on supervised learning, which requires large, labeled datasets that are impractical for distributed sensor networks. A method is needed to learn compact, robust graph representations directly on-device using the abundant unlabeled, streaming data from the graph structure itself.
proposals.hypothesis: A self-supervised learning objective based on reconstructing a latent world model of the graph's dynamics can train highly efficient, quantized GNN architectures that match the representational quality of larger, supervised models for downstream tasks like anomaly detection or state prediction.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework combining spectral sparsification and aggressive post-training quantization of locally connected GNN layers (e.g., GIN or GraphSAGE). The core innovation is a training regime using a joint objective: a contrastive or predictive self-supervised task (inspired by BYOL or Barlow Twins) operating on node embeddings, coupled with a regularization term that enforces the stability of the network's internal predictions of the graph's next state (a lightweight 'world model'). This multi-task loss will be applied during a distillation phase from a teacher network to the final lightweight student.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a recipe for creating ultra-efficient GNNs capable of on-node inference and incremental learning. It will provide empirical evidence that self-supervised objectives derived from world model prediction are particularly effective for stabilizing the training of severely quantized graph networks. The resulting models will be validated on real-time sensor network and IoT graph benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: While model compression for GNNs exists, the integration of a self-supervised, temporal world-modeling objective specifically designed to improve the robustness of highly quantized and pruned graph architectures is novel. This approach moves beyond static graph SSL by explicitly training the compact network to model dynamic node interactions.

ideas.title: Structure-First Feature Learning via Geometric Pretraining
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from structural biology's shift from function-first to structure-first design, this idea inverts the deep learning pretraining paradigm. In protein design, engineers first create a stable structural scaffold and later add function, leading to more robust designs. Adapted to computer vision, we propose pretraining a convolutional network not on image classification, but on a task demanding precise geometric understanding, such as panoramic depth estimation or video object segmentation. This forces the network to learn rich, spatially-aware features about object structure and scene layout. This 'structure-first' backbone is then adapted for image classification, challenging the assumption that classification-derived features are the best starting point for all vision tasks. The hypothesis is that features forged in geometric precision will offer superior robustness, better generalization to downstream tasks requiring localization, and potentially a new path for learning visual representations.

ideas.title: The inverse problem: From detection to image generation
ideas.description: Investigate the dual of the detection/recognition task. Using the learned features and bounding box predictors, could one generate or modify images to contain, remove, or alter objects in a semantically consistent way? This explores the generative capabilities of discriminative features.
ideas.sourceTitle: OverFeat: Integrated Recognition, Localization and Detection using Convolutional Networks

ideas.title: End-to-end joint training for all tasks
ideas.description: The paper notes that classification, localization, and detection were trained separately. A future direction is to develop a unified training objective that jointly optimizes the network for all three tasks simultaneously, potentially improving feature sharing and overall performance.
ideas.sourceTitle: OverFeat: Integrated Recognition, Localization and Detection using Convolutional Networks

ideas.title: Geometric-Semantic Feature Co-Regularization for Unified Localization and Classification
ideas.description: This system inverts the classification-first paradigm by co-designing localization and classification from the ground up, rather than sequentially. A shared backbone network is trained under a dual loss that includes a geometric precision term (e.g., segmentation IoU) and a semantic discrimination term (classification cross-entropy), bridged by a feature consistency regularizer. This forces the learned features to be simultaneously precise for spatial tasks and discriminative for categorical tasks, avoiding the semantic poverty of a purely geometric model. The resulting unified features are superior for downstream geometric tasks like detection and segmentation, while maintaining or exceeding the classification performance of a classification-first network.

ideas.title: Unified Multi-Task Visual Feature Decoding for Textual and Geometric Synthesis
ideas.description: This research proposes a single, scaled network that builds a shared feature pyramid to simultaneously solve a broad set of visual tasks (e.g., classification, depth, normals). The key innovation is the addition of an inverse generative pathway: a character-level decoder conditioned on specific high-level features from this shared pyramid, capable of generating descriptive text (like object names or attributes) that corresponds to the visual predictions. This allows the model to not only perceive and interpret complex visual scenes across multiple geometric and semantic dimensions but also to articulate its understanding in text, providing a direct, interpretable link between the learned visual representations and language.

ideas.title: Multi-Task Learning via a Unified Physics-Inspired Lagrangian
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from classical mechanics and control theory, where a single Lagrangian function unifies multiple system objectives, this idea proposes a novel unified training objective for computer vision. Instead of separate training or fixed loss weighting for classification, localization, and detection, a trainable Lagrangian framework is introduced. The core network is trained end-to-end on a combined loss, where a lightweight, ancillary network dynamically learns and adjusts the weighting coefficients between the task-specific losses. This adaptive mechanism, inspired by optimizing a system under constraints, allows the model to discover an optimal balance for joint optimization, potentially leading to superior feature sharing and overall performance compared to static multi-task learning approaches.

ideas.title: Global Context Primacy for Adaptive Spatial Sampling
ideas.description: This research direction inverts the standard convolutional paradigm by first modeling long-range, global dependencies in the input—whether pixels or characters—using self-attention or similar mechanisms. This global understanding is then used to actively predict a sparse set of salient regions and scales, dynamically allocating computation only to these areas instead of using a dense sliding window. The synergy enables a model that is both computationally efficient and contextually rich, as the initial global analysis provides the necessary guidance to bypass exhaustive local search. This results in an architecture where local feature extraction is subservient to and directed by a primary global representation.

ideas.title: Extreme-Scale Adaptive Receptive Fields for Global Visual Context
ideas.description: This research investigates a novel architecture that treats an entire high-resolution image or video stream as a massive, irregular graph, where pixels or superpixels are nodes. An extreme-scale locally connected network operates directly on this graph, using sparse tensor operations to efficiently propagate information across the entire visual field. Instead of a sliding window, the model's receptive fields adaptively form and hierarchically coalesce based on the input's visual semantics, directly predicting object locations, scales, and identities from the global context. This enables real-time, holistic scene understanding at unprecedented resolutions by fusing the efficiency of sparse, graph-based computation with the goal of eliminating exhaustive multi-scale scanning.

ideas.title: Scaling the number of tasks: A unified network for all visual tasks
ideas.description: Extend the 'single network for multiple tasks' principle beyond classification, localization, and detection. Scale the framework to simultaneously handle a much broader set of visual tasks (e.g., depth estimation, surface normals, keypoints) from a shared feature pyramid.
ideas.sourceTitle: OverFeat: Integrated Recognition, Localization and Detection using Convolutional Networks

ideas.title: Scalable, Lightweight Unified Networks for Massive-Scale On-Device Graph Learning
ideas.description: This research investigates the scaling laws of unified, OverFeat-inspired convolutional architectures (applied to graph-structured data) when trained on massively large and diverse graph datasets, such as web-scale knowledge graphs or sensor network logs. The core innovation is to co-design this scaling exploration with extreme architectural constraints—like binarization and aggressive pruning—from the outset, forcing the learned representations to be inherently efficient. The goal is to discover if scaling data diversity and volume under a strict on-device compute budget leads to emergent, robust, and generalizable 'foundation' models for graphs that are natively deployable in IoT and edge environments. This challenges the conventional paradigm of first training a large model and then compressing it, aiming instead to learn maximally informative yet minimal representations directly from large-scale data.

ideas.title: Gradient-Harmonized, Conflict-Aware Joint Training for Unified Vision Tasks
ideas.description: This work proposes a unified network trained end-to-end for classification, localization, and detection, but directly addresses the core optimization conflict inherent in multi-task learning. Instead of a fixed weighted sum of losses, the system employs a real-time gradient coordination module that analyzes alignment and conflict between task-specific gradients at each training step. Based on this analysis, it applies dynamic gradient modulation—such as projection onto agreed-upon directions or adaptive loss re-weighting—to ensure cooperative optimization. This results in robust feature sharing without destructive interference, consistently outperforming both separate training and naive joint training baselines.

ideas.title: Applying Metabolic Bypass Pathways to Deep Character ConvNets
ideas.description: The original idea proposes transferring residual connections from computer vision (ResNets) to character-level text ConvNets to enable deeper networks. Drawing an analogy from Biochemistry/Metabolic Engineering, where 'bypass pathways' are introduced to circumvent bottlenecks in long metabolic reaction chains, we adapt this solution. In deep character ConvNets, we can design structured, adaptive 'information bypass' connections that function like engineered metabolic shunts. These bypasses would not merely add an input to a layer's output (as in standard residuals) but could employ learned gating or multiplicative interactions to dynamically route information and gradient flow around specific network segments prone to causing training instability. This adaptation aims to more effectively overcome the vanishing gradient problem in the unique context of hierarchical character-to-concept learning, potentially allowing for the construction and successful training of significantly deeper and more powerful character-based models.

ideas.title: Scaling Laws for Unified ConvNets via Ecological Niche Modeling Frameworks
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogy from ecology to explore the scaling laws of the OverFeat integrated framework. In ecology, species distribution models (SDMs) use data across diverse environmental gradients to predict biodiversity scaling. Analogously, we propose treating ConvNet classes as 'species' and datasets as 'biomes.' The adapted research idea is to systematically train OverFeat on nested subsets of data that vary in size and 'ecological diversity'—defined by class similarity and feature variance—across significantly larger and more diverse datasets than ImageNet, including domain-specific ones with many classes. The goal is to fit predictive, quantitative scaling laws that describe how model performance (accuracy), parameter efficiency, and cross-task transferability scale as functions of dataset size and diversity, providing a principled framework for understanding the limits and requirements of scaling unified ConvNet approaches.

ideas.title: Dynamic Multi-Scale Dilated Convolutions with Learned Dilation Gating for Character-Level Modeling
ideas.description: This redesigned system introduces learnable, input-adaptive dilation for character-level convolutional networks to overcome the rigidity of fixed dilation schedules. A gating mechanism, conditioned on the local context, dynamically selects and blends outputs from parallel convolutional branches with different dilation rates. This allows the model to capture character patterns at variable ranges—from local morpheme structures to longer-range dependencies—within the same layer. The approach combines the parameter efficiency of dilated convolutions with the flexibility to model the multi-scale hierarchical nature of language.

ideas.title: Inverse Tomography for Visual Synthesis: Using Discriminative Features as Probes for Generative Inversion
ideas.description: This idea investigates the dual of detection by drawing an analogy to medical tomography. In CT, an internal image is reconstructed from external projection measurements, solving an inverse problem. Similarly, a trained object detector acts as a forward model that 'projects' a pixel image into a space of semantic features and bounding boxes. The analogous solution—tomographic reconstruction algorithms—is adapted here. The research would develop a generative inversion framework where the goal is to synthesize or modify an image such that, when passed through a fixed, pre-trained discriminative detector, it produces a desired set of feature activations and bounding box predictions. This explores using the detector's learned representations as a rigid 'probe' to guide image generation, enabling semantically consistent object insertion, removal, or alteration by inverting the detection process.

ideas.title: Theoretical Framework for Multi-Task Graph Neural Networks: Stability, Generalization, and Adaptive Loss Balancing
ideas.description: This research synthesizes theoretical graph CNN analysis with multi-task learning theory to develop a principled framework for multi-task Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). It aims to derive theoretical conditions under which a GNN, trained on multiple node- or graph-level tasks (e.g., classification and regression), remains stable to perturbations in the graph structure while generalizing across topologies. Crucially, the framework will incorporate formal results from multi-task learning to automatically and adaptively balance the loss functions of the constituent tasks, where the balancing mechanism is explicitly designed to optimize for the derived stability and generalization bounds, creating a closed-loop theory-to-practice system.

ideas.title: Generative Graph Coarsening for Semantic Image Manipulation
ideas.description: This research investigates a novel generative inverse of graph-based pooling for detection. It uses the learned, semantically meaningful graph coarsening hierarchy from a discriminative object detection network as a structural blueprint. By inverting this hierarchy—treating the coarse, community-detected graph representations as a semantic scaffold—the model can guide a generative process to synthesize or modify image regions in a contextually consistent manner. This enables tasks like inserting an object into a scene at a specified node in the graph hierarchy, ensuring it respects the scale, occlusion, and relational semantics defined by the coarsened graph structure, thereby bridging structured scene understanding with controlled image generation.

ideas.title: Theoretical Analysis of Inverse Generation on Graphs via Discriminative Features
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the inverse problem of generating or editing graph-structured data (e.g., molecules, social networks, 3D point clouds) using the learned features and predictors from a discriminative Graph Convolutional Network (GCN). It develops a theoretical framework to analyze the properties of this inverse mapping, specifically its stability to perturbations in the input graph or feature space, its ability to generalize the generative act across different graph topologies, and its approximation capabilities for producing semantically consistent edits. This formally bridges the generative potential of discriminative models with the rigorous analysis of GCN properties, opening a new avenue for controllable, theory-backed generation on non-Euclidean domains.

ideas.title: Multiscale Pooling via Renormalization Group Methods
ideas.description: This idea transfers the conceptual framework of the Renormalization Group (RG) from statistical physics to graph-based deep learning for pooling. In RG, a system (like a spin lattice) is analyzed by iteratively coarse-graining its microscopic degrees of freedom into blocks and deriving an effective Hamiltonian for the new, simpler system, preserving critical phenomena. Adapted to graph pooling, this means designing an algorithm that, at each pooling layer, groups a set of input graph nodes into a super-node (a block) not just based on topology or features alone, but via a learned, parametric procedure that computes a new, effective feature vector and a new, effective adjacency matrix for the coarsened graph. This creates a semantically meaningful hierarchy where each pooled graph level represents the original data at a different scale of relational abstraction, potentially leading to more efficient and interpretable downsampling of graph signals in locally connected network architectures.

ideas.title: Inverse Problem: Graph Topology Inference from Signals
ideas.description: Consider the inverse problem: given a set of observed signals on unknown nodes and the parameters of a trained graph CNN model, can the underlying graph topology be inferred? This scales the problem down to structure discovery from data and model activations.
ideas.sourceTitle: Spectral Networks and Locally Connected Networks on Graphs

ideas.title: Attention-Guided Inverse Graph Inference for Efficient Structure Discovery
ideas.description: This research direction tackles the inverse problem of inferring an unknown graph topology from observed node signals and a trained GNN model. Instead of an exhaustive search over possible edges, it adapts computational models of visual attention—such as saliency maps or reinforcement learning agents—to sequentially and strategically query the most informative potential connections based on the model's current activation patterns and the signal data. This attention mechanism focuses computational resources on promising regions of the vast latent structural space, iteratively refining a candidate graph. The synergy enables scalable topology discovery for complex systems by merging the goal-driven efficiency of cognitive attention with the structural inference challenge of inverse graph learning.

ideas.title: Adapting Quantum Field Theory Renormalization Group Methods for Graph CNN Stability Analysis
ideas.description: This research adapts the Renormalization Group (RG) framework from quantum field theory and statistical physics to develop a novel theoretical analysis of Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNNs). The analogous problem is understanding system stability and universality under scale transformations. The solution involves tracking the flow of effective parameters as degrees of freedom are integrated out. Adapted to GCNNs, we propose to formally define a graph coarse-graining flow (e.g., via successive graph pooling layers or spectral approximations) and analyze the corresponding flow of the network's learned representations and operational parameters. This theoretical framework aims to rigorously characterize: 1) Stability: Classifying graph perturbations as 'relevant' or 'irrelevant' based on their impact on the network's output under the RG flow, providing formal stability guarantees. 2) Generalization: Defining 'universality classes' of graph topologies that share the same fixed point in the parameter flow, explaining when a model trained on one graph type will perform well on another. 3) Approximation Capabilities: Comparing the RG flows of GCNNs and standard CNNs on grid-like graphs to identify fundamental differences in their multi-scale representation learning, thereby delineating their relative expressive powers.

ideas.title: Extension to video and temporal data
ideas.description: The OverFeat framework is applied to static images. A natural extension is to adapt the integrated recognition, localization, and detection approach to video sequences, leveraging temporal coherence for improved accuracy and efficiency.
ideas.sourceTitle: OverFeat: Integrated Recognition, Localization and Detection using Convolutional Networks

ideas.title: Multi-Task Loss Balancing for Scalable Learning on High-Dimensional Heterogeneous Graphs
ideas.description: This research direction proposes extending spectral and locally connected networks to high-dimensional graphs like social networks or 3D meshes, which inherently contain multiple learning tasks (e.g., node classification, link prediction, graph property regression). The key innovation is to integrate theories from multi-task learning to automatically and dynamically balance the losses from these diverse tasks during training. This synergy addresses the core challenge of scaling to high-dimensional graphs: not just computational complexity, but the effective joint optimization of competing objectives on a shared, complex structural representation. The outcome is a unified, scalable framework for heterogeneous graph representation learning that is robust to task imbalance and conflict.

ideas.title: A Unified Multi-Task Visual Network with Task-Specific Modulation Heads
ideas.description: This research expands the 'single network for multiple tasks' principle by scaling it to a much broader set of visual tasks (e.g., depth estimation, surface normals, keypoint detection, segmentation, edge detection) using an analogy from Software-Defined Radio (SDR). In SDR, a single hardware platform uses a common front-end for signal acquisition, with software-defined, modular processing blocks dynamically configured for specific protocols. Adapted to computer vision, the solution involves a unified, shared feature pyramid backbone that extracts a rich, generic visual representation. To this backbone, we attach lightweight, plug-and-play 'Task Modulation Heads.' Each head is a small, specialized neural module that conditions the shared features—via mechanisms like feature-wise linear modulation (FiLM) or adaptive instance normalization—to solve a specific visual task. This architecture allows for the dynamic addition or removal of tasks without retraining the entire backbone, enabling efficient scaling of the framework's capabilities from a single, shared feature foundation.

ideas.title: Transferring Dilated Convolutions from Audio/Speech
ideas.description: Dilated (atrous) convolutions, used in audio and image processing to increase receptive field without losing resolution, could be applied to character-level ConvNets. This would allow the network to capture longer-range character patterns more efficiently than simply stacking many standard convolutional layers.
ideas.sourceTitle: Character-level Convolutional Networks for Text Classification

ideas.title: Task-Modulated Feature Routing: A Sparsely-Gated Unified Network for Diverse Visual Tasks
ideas.description: This redesigned system addresses catastrophic interference in multi-task learning by introducing a sparsely-gated routing mechanism atop a shared feature pyramid. A lightweight, task-conditioned router generates binary masks that selectively activate distinct but overlapping subsets of feature channels for each task head, allowing the model to learn a common feature pool while preventing gradient conflicts. This approach enables positive transfer for related tasks (e.g., depth and normals) while isolating unrelated ones (e.g., classification), scaling effectively to a broad set of visual tasks without significant performance degradation.

ideas.title: Efficient On-Device Graph Structure Discovery for Adaptive Vision
ideas.description: This research fuses graph topology inference with efficient mobile vision architectures. The core idea is to develop a scaled-down, quantized OverFeat-style network that, during its forward pass on a device, also runs a lightweight inverse process on its own intermediate feature signals. This process infers a sparse, task-relevant graph structure (e.g., spatial or semantic relationships between image regions or objects) implicit in the current input. The inferred graph can then be fed back to guide adaptive, efficient computation in subsequent layers or frames, such as pruning message-passing operations or focusing attention, creating a self-optimizing, context-aware vision system for embedded applications.

ideas.title: Using multi-task learning theory for loss balancing
ideas.description: The paper trains different tasks (classification, regression) on the same features. Theories and methods from multi-task learning research could be transferred to automatically balance the loss functions from each task, optimizing shared feature learning.
ideas.sourceTitle: OverFeat: Integrated Recognition, Localization and Detection using Convolutional Networks

ideas.title: Crystallography-Inspired Graph Topology Inference from Model Diffraction Patterns
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core inverse problem from X-ray crystallography to graph topology inference. In crystallography, a crystal's unknown 3D structure is deduced from its 2D diffraction pattern by solving the phase problem. Analogously, here, the set of observed node signals and the known parameters (e.g., filter weights) of a pre-trained graph CNN are treated as the measurable 'diffraction pattern'—the output of the underlying graph structure being transformed by the model. The adapted solution involves developing reconstruction algorithms that treat the model's forward pass as a scattering operation. By formulating constraints based on the spectral properties of the GCNN filters and the sparsity/connectivity of real-world graphs, iterative projection methods (similar to charge flipping or hybrid input-output algorithms) can be designed to solve for the adjacency matrix, effectively performing 'structure discovery' from data and model activations alone.

ideas.title: End-to-End Learnable Graph Attention Networks for Adaptive Proposal Merging
ideas.description: This approach frames the box merging problem as a node classification task on a fully-connected graph of candidate bounding boxes. A neural module, such as a Transformer or Graph Attention Network, processes visual and geometric features from each box to compute attention-weighted messages between nodes. This allows the model to learn which boxes should influence each other and how, directly from data. The system is trained end-to-end with the upstream detector, enabling it to adapt its merging strategy to specific object types, scales, and occlusion patterns without manual CRF design.

ideas.title: Meta-Compressed Character Models: Few-Shot Task Adaptation for Micro-Controllers
ideas.description: This research develops a framework for creating ultra-tiny character-level models that are not just compressed for a single task, but are inherently adaptable. The core innovation is a meta-learning pipeline that produces a foundational, compressed model capable of generating task-specific parameters via a lightweight, on-device adaptation step using few-shot learning. This allows a single deployed model to handle multiple text classification tasks (e.g., sentiment, topic, intent) as needed by the application, overcoming the brittleness of static single-task compression. The system co-optimizes for extreme compression, efficient adaptation runtime, and robust few-shot performance across a defined task distribution.

ideas.title: Extreme Scale-Down via Synthetic Minimal Genome Design
ideas.description: This research adapts the concept of designing a minimal synthetic genome from biology to the problem of creating ultra-compact character models. In synthetic biology, a minimal functional genome is created by identifying and synthesizing only the genes essential for life in a given environment, discarding all redundancy. We propose to solve the model scale-down problem analogously: for a specific text classification task and target microcontroller, we will define a 'minimal functional architecture' and use a neural architecture search (NAS) or evolutionary algorithm to systematically synthesize a tiny character-level ConvNet from scratch. This algorithm will identify the absolutely essential architectural components—such as critical filter sizes, minimal layer depth, and necessary character embedding dimensions—required to meet the performance threshold. The result is a purpose-built, ultra-efficient model that is synthesized for minimal memory and compute footprint from its inception, rather than being a pruned or distilled version of a larger, potentially over-provisioned network.

ideas.title: Spectral Approximation for Efficient Long-Range Dependencies in Large-Alphabet Sequence Models
ideas.description: This research direction fuses scalable spectral approximation techniques with the challenge of processing long sequences over large alphabets. It proposes to model long-range dependencies in sequences—represented as massive, structured graphs where nodes are tokens and edges capture complex, multi-scale relationships—using approximated spectral filters derived from methods like Chebyshev polynomials or the Nyström method. This avoids the intractable cost of full eigen-decomposition on these large implicit graphs while enabling efficient, globally-informed convolutional operations across thousands of tokens. The outcome is a novel neural architecture capable of handling rich Unicode text and book-length documents with significantly improved parameter and computational efficiency compared to hierarchical local convolutions or attention-based models.

ideas.title: Adapting Multi-Resolution Analysis from Astrophysics for High-Dimensional Graph Neural Networks
ideas.description: The original idea proposes extending spectral and locally connected network frameworks to high-dimensional graphs like social networks or 3D meshes. Drawing an analogy from astrophysics, where scientists analyze immense, high-dimensional spatial datasets (e.g., galaxy distributions) using multi-resolution and adaptive mesh refinement techniques to manage scale and focus on informative regions. The adapted research idea is to develop a hierarchical graph neural network that first constructs a multi-scale representation of the high-dimensional input graph via an initial clustering phase. The spectral/local network operations are then applied adaptively across these scales, enabling the model to efficiently capture both global structure and local detail, thereby testing and significantly extending the scalability and performance limits of the original methods on very large, complex graphs.

ideas.title: Adaptive Spectral Networks for Dynamic Topology Graphs via Fluid Dynamics Analogies
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of a fixed graph structure by developing spectral or locally connected networks for graphs with dynamic topology. The analogy is drawn from Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), specifically the Arbitrary Lagrangian-Eulerian (ALE) method used to simulate flows with moving boundaries by dynamically re-meshing the domain and interpolating solution fields. Adapting this solution, we propose a novel framework for dynamic graph networks. The core of the adapted idea is to develop a method that dynamically 're-meshes' the graph's spectral or latent representation as nodes and edges evolve over time. This involves creating a core adaptive operator that continuously interpolates node features and the graph's spectral basis (e.g., eigenvectors of the graph Laplacian) from the previous topology state to the new one. This approach aims to enable stable, long-term learning on evolving graphs—such as communication or biological interaction networks—by maintaining temporal consistency in the learned representations and preventing catastrophic forgetting, significantly expanding the applicability of graph neural networks to truly dynamic domains.

ideas.title: Multiscale, Aspect-Ratio Invariant Object Detection via Graph Wavelet Representations
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a novel object detection architecture that abandons fixed input aspect ratios by treating each region proposal as an irregular graph of visual features, preserving object geometry. By applying principles of multiresolution analysis and wavelet theory on graphs, the network employs locally supported spectral filters to perform convolutions directly on these variable-shaped graphs. This enables multi-scale feature extraction within each region without distorting its native aspect ratio. The synergy yields a model that is inherently robust to object shape and scale variation, improving localization accuracy by analyzing visual structure at multiple resolutions within its true geometric context.

ideas.title: Inverting the classification-first paradigm
ideas.description: OverFeat uses a network trained for classification as a base for localization and detection. One could invert this: start with a network optimized for precise localization or segmentation, and then adapt it for classification, potentially improving features for geometric tasks.
ideas.sourceTitle: OverFeat: Integrated Recognition, Localization and Detection using Convolutional Networks

ideas.title: Differentiable and Task-Adaptive Graph Pooling via Learned Coarsening
ideas.description: This research develops a novel graph pooling layer where the coarsening hierarchy is not pre-computed but learned jointly with the network parameters. A lightweight, differentiable module processes the current node features and graph structure to produce a soft assignment matrix, which defines how nodes are merged at each pooling step. This approach allows the pooling operation to discover and adapt hierarchical structures that are semantically meaningful specifically for the target task, such as node or graph classification. The system is trained end-to-end, ensuring the pooling strategy optimizes the final objective rather than an intermediate structural metric, leading to more robust and performant hierarchical graph representations.

ideas.title: Applying Seismic Interferometry's Virtual Source Concept to Dilated Character Convolutions
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept of transferring dilated convolutions to character-level ConvNets by drawing an analogy from geophysics. In seismology, seismic interferometry solves the problem of limited physical sensor arrays by using cross-correlation to create virtual sources, thereby extending the effective sensing range and resolution. Adapting this solution, we propose a novel convolutional block for character sequences. The block uses a standard convolution as a 'physical receiver' for local patterns and a parallel dilated convolution as a 'virtual receiver.' Through a designed interaction mechanism (inspired by cross-correlation), the block synthesizes features representing longer-range character relationships, effectively increasing the receptive field and capturing patterns like multi-word expressions or clause structures within a compact architecture. This provides a more parameter- and compute-efficient method for modeling long-range character dependencies than simply stacking many standard layers.

ideas.title: Multiscale Graph Convolutions via Geographic Wavelet Pyramids
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from Geographic Information Systems (GIS) for multi-resolution terrain analysis using wavelet pyramids on regular grids to the problem of defining locally supported, multi-scale filters for Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNNs). The analogous cartographic solution constructs a Laplacian pyramid by recursively low-pass filtering and downsampling a digital elevation model, creating a scale hierarchy where wavelet coefficients represent local detail. To adapt this, we construct a graph wavelet pyramid by applying a graph coarsening algorithm (e.g., Graclus) to recursively generate a hierarchy of coarser versions of the original graph. At each level, we define graph wavelet filters as operations that capture the signal difference between a graph and its coarser counterpart, analogous to the detail coefficients in a cartographic pyramid. These wavelet filters provide the locally supported, spectral-like filters needed for a multi-scale GCNN architecture, directly addressing the global support limitation of traditional spectral graph filters by tying filter support to localized regions within and across scales of the graph hierarchy.

ideas.title: Efficient OverFeat via Chiplet-Style Modular Pruning and Heterogeneous Quantization
ideas.description: This research adapts the modular, heterogeneous design principles from microprocessor and System-on-a-Chip engineering to create a highly efficient version of the OverFeat architecture for mobile/embedded vision. The analogous solution from chip design involves disaggregating a complex monolithic system into specialized, independently optimized functional blocks (chiplets/cores) connected by an efficient fabric. Adapted to neural networks, this means developing an automated framework to decompose the OverFeat architecture into distinct functional modules based on their role in the feature hierarchy. Each module (e.g., initial convolutions, complex feature extractors, fully-connected classifiers) is then treated as a 'neural chiplet' and subjected to a tailored optimization strategy—such as aggressive 4-bit quantization for early layers, feature-map distillation for intermediate layers, and structured pruning for final layers. Finally, these optimized heterogeneous modules are reintegrated using trained, lightweight connection layers that mimic a high-efficiency interconnect, resulting in a scaled-down OverFeat network that maintains critical accuracy while achieving drastic reductions in computational cost, memory footprint, and power consumption for real-time operation on resource-constrained devices.

ideas.title: Extreme Scale-Down: Tiny Character Models for Micro-Controllers
ideas.description: Scale down the character-level ConvNet architecture for deployment on resource-constrained devices (e.g., mobile phones, IoT). This involves creating ultra-compact models via pruning, quantization, or knowledge distillation that maintain performance on specific text classification tasks with minimal memory and compute footprint.
ideas.sourceTitle: Character-level Convolutional Networks for Text Classification

ideas.title: Spectral Synthesis: Text Generation from Learned Character Feature Spectra
ideas.description: This idea applies the inverse problem methodology of astrophysical spectroscopy to text generation. In spectroscopy, one infers composition from a spectrum and synthesizes a spectrum from a known composition. Here, the analogous 'spectrum' is the high-level feature representation extracted by a character-level classification ConvNet. The adapted solution is to build a generative model that acts as a 'spectral synthesizer.' It uses the ConvNet's learned character-level feature maps as a fixed library of compositional primitives. Conditioned on a target feature vector (representing a desired semantic or stylistic 'state'), the decoder performs a synthesis process, akin to radiative transfer, to generate a coherent sequence of characters that, when passed back through the ConvNet, would ideally produce a similar high-level feature representation. This explores what the model has learned by attempting to reconstruct or generate novel text from its own internal feature 'spectra.'

ideas.title: Topological Field Theory for Signal Processing on Complex Domains
ideas.description: This research seed proposes a radical generalization of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) from graphs to arbitrary non-Euclidean domains like manifolds, simplicial complexes, and hypergraphs. Drawing an analogy from Theoretical Physics, specifically Topological Quantum Field Theory (TQFT), we note a parallel: TQFT formulates physical laws that are invariant under continuous deformation of the underlying space, relying on algebraic structures assigned to topological features rather than a fixed geometry. The analogous solution is TQFT's axiomatic framework, which uses category theory to map topological cobordisms to algebraic operations.
